Hi @mcornella 
I'm using the `ys` theme with `zplug`, and after I updated it I started to get this error

```
zsh: command not found: svn_prompt_info
```
To fix it I have to explicitly install `svn` plugin.  It's possible to fix it in a better way? (for instance, adding a configuration option to "disable" svn prompt?)

Thanks :)

body: Such as only enabling it if svn is installed.

Yes, it's what I did, but since I don't need svn support, I wondered whether there is a better solution than installing the svn plugin.

author: szaffarano

body: You don't need the svn plugin, the `lib/prompt_info_functions.zsh` file adds noop definitions for these so they don't run anything unless the plugin is enabled.

```
zplug "lib/prompt_info_functions", from:oh-my-zsh
```
to my `.zshrc` and it worked

body:
Hi @bang-eoh — unfortunately I don't use WSL or WSL2 and so cannot speak much to the method of determining that environment. I have some comments, notes, and I hope they're helpful.

Couldn't this be solved by setting your own `PATH` to resolve to the correct git executable, instead? Or export `GIT_EXEC_PATH`?

  * The hard-coded `/mnt/c/…` path is a little alarming. Wouldn't this also trigger for people running Linux directly, with a mounted Windows partition? They could not run `git.exe`, but this would forcibly attempt that. And wouldn't it fail to trigger for people with, e.g. a `D:\` data drive in the same situation as you? This seems like such specific heuristic. There are many other `git` invocations in `ohmyzsh`, but they don't do anything similar.

  * In general, if you need to check a path, it would be better to compare with a glob, not `grep`. For example:
    ```zsh
      if [[ "${PWD:A}" == /mnt/c/* ]]; then
          …
     ````

  * Note that regex syntax is different than globs:
    * The backslashes in that regex are redundant, because forward-slashes don't require escaping.
    * The asterisk in the regex `/*` means "with zero or more forward slashes", _not_ "followed by anything".

body:
**Same as posted in issue:**

1 suggestion I would make is to use `brew --prefix` to determine the install folder/postgres location, this returns for me:
- on Apple Silicon: `/opt/homebrew`
- on Intel: `/usr/local`

and then append `/var/postgres`

That way in the future if ever things change again, the plugin will likely keep working if the defaults change along.

Sample:
`alias startpost="pg_ctl -D $(brew --prefix)/var/postgres -l $(brew --prefix)/var/postgres/server.log start"`

body:
If anyone asks how to use it then do the next:
Add `conda-env` to plugins in your `~/.zshrc`:
`plugins=(... conda-env)`
run `source ~/.zshrc.`
Then if you run :
`╰─❯ conda_prompt_info`
`[abs]`
It will say the name of your current environment.

body:
@ThemysciraData I tested the fix on centos 6 with `docker run -it centos:6`.

<details>
<summary>Side note: I had to fix the mirrorlist first by [using the CentOS Vault](https://www.getpagespeed.com/server-setup/how-to-fix-yum-after-centos-6-went-eol)</summary>

### Broken yum
```console
[root@268795c46af9 ~]# yum update
Loaded plugins: fastestmirror, ovl
Setting up Update Process
Determining fastest mirrors
YumRepo Error: All mirror URLs are not using ftp, http[s] or file.
 Eg. Invalid release/repo/arch combination/
removing mirrorlist with no valid mirrors: /var/cache/yum/x86_64/6/base/mirrorlist.txt
Error: Cannot find a valid baseurl for repo: base
```

### Fix: use CentOS Vault
```console
[root@268795c46af9 ~]# cat <<-'EOF' > /etc/yum.repos.d/CentOS-Base.repo
[C6.10-base]
name=CentOS-6.10 - Base
baseurl=http://vault.centos.org/6.10/os/$basearch/
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-CentOS-6
enabled=1
metadata_expire=never

[C6.10-updates]
name=CentOS-6.10 - Updates
baseurl=http://vault.centos.org/6.10/updates/$basearch/
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-CentOS-6
enabled=1
metadata_expire=never

[C6.10-extras]
name=CentOS-6.10 - Extras
baseurl=http://vault.centos.org/6.10/extras/$basearch/
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-CentOS-6
enabled=1
metadata_expire=never

[C6.10-contrib]
name=CentOS-6.10 - Contrib
baseurl=http://vault.centos.org/6.10/contrib/$basearch/
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-CentOS-6
enabled=0
metadata_expire=never

[C6.10-centosplus]
name=CentOS-6.10 - CentOSPlus
baseurl=http://vault.centos.org/6.10/centosplus/$basearch/
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-CentOS-6
enabled=0
metadata_expire=never
EOF
```

</details>

I had to fix the `git fetch` command which behaved differently in v1.7.1 than in my newer version. After that, centos:6 does install Oh My Zsh correctly.

I'm going to merge this, but do note that there are many other issues with git support in other areas (the first I found, is using `--short` in `git symbolic-ref`, which wasn't published [until v1.7.10](https://github.com/git/git/commit/42b00599be684e20462c431a718f86c2b6fce575)). In any case, that was almost 10 years ago so you should try to do as much as possible to get out of CentOS 6, or get a more recent git version as documented in [Larson's workaround](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/8533#issuecomment-646082616).

Only nitpick is that it might be useful to print the full path to the .zshrc file in the "Found .zshrc ..." log messages so that it's more obvious which file was found and where. i.e., on line 313 (https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/ebfd5eba641697d4b96d8f7dba9c53650c731ee4#diff-5dc310979be51acc76eb748ef379630b65614b5a4132fc5a870019ade043935bR313) and line 328 (https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/ebfd5eba641697d4b96d8f7dba9c53650c731ee4#diff-5dc310979be51acc76eb748ef379630b65614b5a4132fc5a870019ade043935bR328) print `Found $zdot/.zshrc.`

I think it would be beneficial to merge this PR even before any corresponding changes to the uninstall script are made. The uninstall script already ignores the `ZSH` variable and tries to delete `~/.oh-my-zsh` if it exists. In my opinion, uninstalling oh-my-zsh at non-default paths should be a separate PR from this one. Thanks for all the help on this!

author: NeilGirdhar

body:
Hi @NeilGirdhar,
Sorry for the late response. We developed a [brand new policy](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#new-plugin-aliases) related to accepting or not new aliases 
I would say this PR doesn't fit that new policy. There are a lot of specific aliases and I'm not sure how many people would use them. Could you please check it and provide the information about workflows and all the stuff, and tell me if you think that I'm wrong (it could perfectly be!) and this PR fits the new policy.
Thanks, and sorry another time for the late response :)
author: carlosala

body:
Thanks for taking a look, @carlosala .  The policy makes perfect sense.  However, I don't think it applies to most of these aliases.

I'll start by sharing some statistics based on my usage.  Running
```sh
history | awk '{print $2}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr    
```
Note that I have history set to only add unique, so consider that my most used commands are more used than reported.  Out of 4292 lines, my ordering is:
```
   1338 findpy  # my alias to search python files (over-represented because the search string varies so much)
    205 g  # git
    183 gvim
    148 replace  # my alias to search and replace
    132 cd
    118 gc  # git commit
    106 glog  # git log
     87 rm
     85 gsw  # git switch branch
     79 cat
     78 gca  # git commit all
     59 sudo
     52 poetry
     52 l
     51 grbi  # git interactive rebase
     49 pipi  # pip install  (PROPOSED)
     48 open
     44 mypy
     44 gbl   # git blame
     42 pipu  # pip update (PROPOSED)
     42 gclg
     39 pytest
     37 mv
     36 gd
     36 find
     35 gswc  # git switch create
     33 pipun  # pip uninstall (PROPOSED)
     32 grbo  # git rebase
     31 grs  # git reset
     31 gbD  # git delete branch
     30 gsh  # git show
     30 agi  # apt get install
     25 pyright
     24 git
     22 grhh  # git hard reset
     22 alias
     21 pylint
     21 gp  # git push
     19 gloga  # git log all
     18 gcp  # git cherry pick
     18 ga  # git add
     17 mkdir
     17 cp
     16 pipig  # pip install from github (PROPOSED)
     15 zathura
     15 py  # python
     14 grep
     14 gm  # git merge
     13 export
     13 cmm_demo  # personal research project
     12 reorder-python-imports
     12 gsta  # git stash
     12 grh  # git reset
     12 gbd  # git delete branch
     11 vim
     10 pip
      9 pipgi  # pip grep install (PROPOSED)
      9 gpg
      9 dm  # personal script
      8 pyenv
      8 history
```
I've added comments to explain the different commands.

As  you can see, four of my proposed aliases are among my most used commands.  I suspect that Python programmers will share similar usage patterns.  This is because these commands subtend very common tasks in Python development.

Before I explain why, let me first say that Python has moved on from some of the aliases in this file.  In particular, "requirements.txt" is outdated.  The Python ecosystem is moving towards modern package and dependency management tools, the most popular of which is [Poetry](https://python-poetry.org/).  And as such, "requirements.txt" has been supplanted by "pyproject.toml".  Thus, I suggest removing the "pipreq" and "pipir" aliases in accordance with your policy.

I do think "pipupall" should stay, but as a user of modern package management, it's not something I ever use.  This is because modern package managers don't simply update everything to the newest version like "pipupall" does.  They have to resolve all dependencies and then solve for a set of *compatible versions*.  So, "pipupall" is antiquated.  I use "poetry update". 

These modern package management tools are the reason for these new commands.  These tools all create a "virtual environment".  These isolated environments can be modified using pip.  The various commands I proposed are ways of editing these environments.  It is no longer the case that you simply "update all versions to latest".  Below, I examine my proposed additions:

pipi: Install packages
---
I mainly use this to temporarily add libraries or tools to a project's virtual environment.  For example, adding a tool like MyPy to check types.   Or adding a library like JaxOpt to see if it solves my problem.

Another very common task is installing a local library in editable mode "pipi -e ..."  You do this when you want to work on the local library and verify that its changes work (as you edit) in a separate local project.

pipig: Install package from GitHub repository
---
This task is not as common as the rest, but the command is sufficiently harry that it's practically impossible to reproduce from memory.  I mainly use this when a tool like MyPy crashes.  Then, I call `pipig mypy mypy` to install from master to see if the master version also crashes.  If it does, I report an issue.

pipigb: Install package from GitHub branch
---
My most recent usage of this was `pipigb numpy numpy maintenance/1.22.x` to see if my problem would be solved in an upcoming version.

pipigp: Install package from GitHub pull request
---
This is used to see if someone else's pull request will solve your problem.  For example, if someone has a PR for MyPy, and you want to see if MyPy will work for you after that PR.

pipu: Upgrade packages
---
I use this all the time to temporarily try the latest version of things in my virtual environment

pipun: Uninstall packages
---
I use this after installing things temporarily to remove them.

pipgi: Grep through installed packages
---
This is how I check what's currently installed before calling "pipi" or "pipu".

piplo: List outdated packages
---
This is how I check the virtual environment for what's outdated.  This is useful because even after "poetry update", there can be many outdated packages due to conflicts.  Nothing can be done locally about that.  If you want newer versions, you can first check why the older version is installed using "poetry show -t" and then check the project whose dependencies are causing the stall for any relevant issue (adding one if necessary).

Hope this helps explain why these commands are useful to more people than just me!  Please let me know what you think.
author: NeilGirdhar

body:
This looks good, but is there a reason to remove the `-C` flag in the `compinit -u` case? If you remove it, then even in that case `compaudit` is called, which I'd think is unnecessary.

If not, then the `grep` call you added could be moved into the `compinit -i` section and used to set `zcompdump_refresh`.
Also, unless I'm misunderstanding something, I believe the `grep` call could just be `grep -q -Fx "$zcompdump_revision"`, which should be a little faster.

Lastly, since this `handle_completion_insecurities` is running in the background, would it make sense to actually run `handle_completion_insecurities` in `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX == True` case as well? Since AFAIU the reason to disable this is a runtime penalty, but background-processing largely eliminates that.

Perhaps a third option for `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX` could be added and enabled by default, which does `compinit -C` _and_ a background `handle_completion_insecurities`?
author: fice-t

body:
Hi @fice-t!

As stated [here](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Completion-System.html#Use-of-compinit), using the `-u` flag in `compinit` will not run `compaudit` to check for potential security issues in completion files. The reason to remove `-C` flag is that, in case of having it, it will blindly follow `.zcompdump` file, without checking if there are new completion functions in present directories at `$fpath`.

Secondly, that `grep` call could not be moved to `compinit -i` part because in case there are new completion functions, also `compinit -u` will update the `.zcompdump` file and potentially remove the `#omz` part. I didn't knew that it would be faster to check if the whole line is present, but it makes sense as if the first character doesn't match it can pass to the next line. Thanks for the suggestion, i'll change that for sure!

The reason to not run always `handle_completion_insecurities` are mainly two. The first one is what you say, startup time, and this PR solves that basically because runs in background, without stopping the sourcing and execution of other parts of zsh startup. The second one is that many users reported that with MacOS it detects some problems related to `root` user, because there is an `admin` user that has the same function as `root` but its name is different, and a safe directory is detected as non-safe (I don't really know that, as I'm a Linux user).

And lastly, with this PR people is not going to have problems nor slow startup using the default approach (without enabling `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX`). This alternative approach will only be useful for those MacOS users that have been reporting issues.

Thanks for your feedback, I really appreciate that! By the way, what do you think about what I'm saying, is everything right, do you think I can improve something?

body:
I agree with Carlo, on empty information the common behavior in prompt functions is to not show anything. You can exit the function with a check for empty content:
```zsh
[[ -n "$current_ctx" ]] || return
```

body:
Now I see that `--no-gpg-sign` was not introduced [until v2.27.0](https://github.com/git/git/commit/c241371c0445cee7116e36bbacb5b35069a81e64). In older versions the argument doesn't work, and in much older versions (e.g. v1.7.1) the rebase command exits with an error.

Instead we should locally change `commit.gpgsign` before the rebase. We should avoid `git -c commit.gpgsign=false` because `git -c` has a similar compatibility problem.

> I also recently found out tha there is `/run/.containerenv` available for toolbox users. Which returns bunch of metadata about the container. Perhaps an option to display the toolbox name alongside the icon would be a good idea `eval $(cat /run/.containerenv) && echo $name` .

author: mcornella

body:
> > I also recently found out tha there is `/run/.containerenv` available for toolbox users. Which returns bunch of metadata about the container. Perhaps an option to display the toolbox name alongside the icon would be a good idea `eval $(cat /run/.containerenv) && echo $name` .
> > I'll have a look at this tomorrow as it got me really excited ;)
> 
> Can you provide a sample .containerenv to see the format?

Sure thing it's 

```
cat /run/.containerenv 
engine="podman-3.4.4"
name="fedora-toolbox-35"
id="362b87172756af906fd8e4bd144fb6311baa0093ebbed7bbe7afdd6a9a851c41"
image="registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-toolbox:35"
imageid="40b181c70b7342032df0b13c16beaf18a3ea7f73617858923c3972a02139d6e8"
rootless=1
```

Where name is the toolbox container name.

I believe this is a generic feature of podman. Although I've been told it only works on privileged containers ( which fedora toolbx is )

From https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html

> Additionally, a container environment file is created in each container to indicate to programs they are running in a container. This file is located at /run/.containerenv. When using the --privileged flag the .containerenv contains name/value pairs indicating the container engine version, whether the engine is running in rootless mode, the container name and id, as well as the image name and id that the container is based on.

body:
I like the idea of `gt` for `git tag`, but I’m not sold on the others.

`git push --tags` pushes *all* tags, which can lead to accidents. Using [`--follow-tags`](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-push#Documentation/git-push.txt---follow-tags) pushes only annotated tags associated with the current commit, which is preferable for many workflows. It can also be enabled by default with the `push.followTags` option, so there's no need for an alias change there.

`gshs` for `git show --stat` is not tag-related. Perhaps useful, but there are a bunch of other extra-detail-adding options on `git show` that seem similarly important...

What's your opinion about it?
Thanks for your contribution! 😊

author: kaivio

body: I manipulate my path as well. Code I write goes into a project path that further has debug, release, and install components before going into the actual install area. My tool for that does: pathtool -h addpath : add current directory to path bugpath N : use /debug/ in path rlspath N : use /release/ in path gldpath N : use /install/ in path cleanpath : remove missing and duplicate path elements delpath N : remove path ordpath O N : re-order path element dopath N : renable path gop N : go to path loadpath : load saved paths nopath N : disable path showpath : show paths the commands that "change" the path only outputs the new path to the terminal, using an alias wrapper to set it for the current shell. Many of the options get the path to change by using a program name and finding with path element it exists in.
author: rwmitchell

body:
You can run these by using the full or relative path, you don't need to change `$PATH`. For example:
```console
$ wget https://example.com/some-command -O ~/Downloads
$ chmod +x ~/Downloads/some-command
$ ~/Downloads/some-command <arguments>
```

You could also add the current working directory to the path with
```sh
path+=(.)
```
and then you can run commands in the current directory without prefixing with `./` (note that if you do this, the recommended approach is to put the current working directory last, so as to not override system commands.

----

Regardless of that, while I appreciate the contribution this is not a thing that common users will need. You can still set it up as a custom plugin, see [adding a new plugin](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization#adding-a-new-plugin). You can also create a repository for the plugin and add it to the list of [External plugins](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/External-plugins).

body:
Hi Adam! Thanks for the PR! Given that this is a breaking change we'll have to add a `BREAKING CHANGE` note so that users update to CLI 2. [These would be the upgrade docs](https://developer.1password.com/docs/cli/upgrade) that we link to, right? Something like:

```
refactor(1password)!: rewrite `opswd` to support 1password CLI 2 (#10787)

BREAKING CHANGE: this commit changes the `opswd` function to use the new
1password CLI 2 arguments. If you're using CLI 1 (check with `op --version`)
you're going to need to update to the new version.
See https://developer.1password.com/docs/cli/upgrade for instructions.
```

Otherwise, if we want to keep backward compatibility with v1 we could redefine the `opwsd` function depending on `op --version` (doing it differently will add complexity to the function which will be very difficult to maintain). We can leverage the `is-at-least` function for that:

```zsh
function opswd {
  # new opswd function
}

# TODO (date): remove old code
autoload -Uz is-at-least
if ! is-at-least 2.0.0 "$(op --version)"; then
  function opswd {
    # old opswd function for v1
  }
fi
```

This other approach will enable us to not add the breaking change message.
author: mcornella

body:
Hey Marc! That is the correct documentation, yes.

My _personal_ preference would be to encourage folks upgrade to the new version, but I am a bit biased here. I am also certainly sympathetic to anyone needing time to make the change.

Sorry for jumping in here: I would really recommend adding the version check since (at least on Mac) the CLI 2 isn't fully supported because it's partly dependent on 1PW 8 – which is as far as I know still in Beta for Mac.

So the version check seems like a legit way to support both - users wanna have the shiny new features and users that wanna stay more on the saver side and wait until the 1PW 8 has a stable version.

body: I'm not arguing against being gender inclusive.  I'm arguing against pushing it blindly without any thought.  Did this bot even have any on-point changes?  I'm assuming Rob Russell chose his own pronouns, which is rather the point of being gender inclusive - this bot indiscriminately recommended changing his chosen pronouns - that is over stepping and should not be tolerated or supported. author: rwmitchell

body:
> Did this bot even have any on-point changes?

No changes were perfect to be applied, but it highlighted some issues: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/e079babdce1f41a6c85296fba7df1672d98e04bd.

> that is over stepping and should not be tolerated

It's a badly written bot. Perhaps creating an issue would've been better. That said, it's a one-time PR that has been kinda useful, so why shouldn't it be tolerated?

> Some of the changes were even more offensive.

Why are you taking offense at a text change? The bot is not telling you that [what is written is wrong](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/10814#issue-1187105993):

> These are not always perfect, but we hope they will assist maintainers in finding and fixing issues

It's a type of bot that is very difficult to write. I took it as an automated search and replace that you can review, which is similar to typo fix drive-by contributions. It is not that uncommon in OSS projects.

I do find it bad that it doesn't have a repo to report issues. And that's all the discussion that this matter needs.

Hi! We are developing a brand new alias policy where we are going to be more "strict" in how we accept aliases, due to the massive amount of them that we have in the project. One of the points is that we are going to accept one generic alias instead of several specific ones. In this case, `go mod` would be the generic one and `go mod download`, for example, the specific one. You can check that policy (is still being finished) [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/11045).

body:
I think we need to make this a zsh check to account for dynamic alias definitions and function conflicts (i.e. "aliases" that are defined as functions, like #10851). A way to do this could be to run zsh, then parse the output of `alias` and `print -l ${(k)functions}`, though this could potentially be super slow if we need to source all plugins.

The problem of side-effects and running extra code (things not related to aliases and functions) would need to be considered as well. I'll think carefully about this last part.

Good start though, love that it contains tests!
author: mcornella

body:
Is there an easy way to source all plugins? Then I would give it a try to see how the runtime will behave.

The current implementation already found a few duplicates. How should we proceed here? I could create a separate issue for every plugin to kickof a discussion for resolving the name conflict.
author: hofbi

body:
@mcornella How should we proceed here? I could create a separate issue for every plugin to kickoff a discussion for resolving the name conflict. We anyhow have to resolve the name conflicts before we can use the CI check in a reasonable way.

And how to proceed with the implementation. Since this already found some conflicts and might prevent future ones, we could already integrate it and improve with a follow up PR that parses the output of `alias` and `print -l ${(k)functions}`. What do you think?
author: hofbi

author: hofbi

body:
I'd push for a zsh check, so we can check also functions. Another problem might be that some aliases are defined only if some binary is present in `$PATH`. Maybe our best option is to go for regex checking all the files looking for `alias <word>`, `function <word> (or <words>)`, `<word> () {` and then if there are two with the same name.
What do you think? @hofbi @mcornella
author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala Agreed.

My suggestion on how to proceed would be to go with this check for now and fix everything that was catched so far. Once this is done, we can extend the check for functions and everything else that comes to our mind.

I am also starting list here with alias collisions we need to solve and my suggestions/ideas to resolve them:

1. Alias `ma=meteor add` defined in `meteor.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `ma=mongocli atlas` in `mongocli.plugin.zsh`. --> My suggestion, rename all `mongocli` from `m` to `mc` which results in `mca`
2. Alias `db=deno bundle` defined in `deno.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `db=dotnet build` in `dotnet.plugin.zsh`. --> My suggestion, rename all `dotnet` from `d` to `dn` which results in `dnb`
3. Alias `gcd=git checkout $(git config gitflow.branch.develop)` defined in `git-flow.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `gcd=git checkout $(git_develop_branch)` in `git.plugin.zsh`. --> Can we use the git plugin function in the git-flow function?
4. Alias `map=web_search duckduckgo \!m` defined in `web-search.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `map=meteor add-platform` in `meteor.plugin.zsh`. --> Rename `map` to `maps`?
5. Alias `sd=ruby script/server --debugger` defined in `rails.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `sd=ruby script/destroy` in `rails.plugin.zsh`. --> This is under legacy stuff so maybe consider removing it?
6. Alias `sp=ruby script/plugin` defined in `rails.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `sp=web_search startpage` in `web-search.plugin.zsh`. --> Also one in rails legacy so consider removing it
7. Alias `rs=repo sync` defined in `repo.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `rs=rails server` in `rails.plugin.zsh`.
8. Alias `ru=repo upload` defined in `repo.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `ru=rails runner` in `rails.plugin.zsh`.
9. Alias `n="$GREP" -Rvi` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `n=nanoc` in `nanoc.plugin.zsh`.
10. Alias `a=echo >>` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `a=ansible ` in `ansible.plugin.zsh`.
11. Alias `c=cat` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `c=composer` in `composer.plugin.zsh`.
12. Alias `p="$PAGER"` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `p=ps -f` in `common-aliases.plugin.zsh`.
13. Alias `m=man` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `m=meteor run` in `meteor.plugin.zsh`. --> Rename `m` to `mr`?
14. Alias `sf="$ROOT" "$GREP" -Rli` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `sf=`_symfony_console`` in `symfony2.plugin.zsh`.
15. Alias `sc="$ROOT" cat` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `sc=ruby script/console` in `rails.plugin.zsh`.
16. Alias `sp="$ROOT" "$PAGER"` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `sp=web_search startpage` in `web-search.plugin.zsh`.
17. Alias `sd="$ROOT" "$WGET"` defined in `singlechar.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `sd=ruby script/destroy` in `rails.plugin.zsh`.
18. Alias `afs=apt-file search --regexp` defined in `ubuntu.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `afs=apt-file search --regexp` in `debian.plugin.zsh`.
19. Alias `allpkgs=dpkg --get-selections | grep -v deinstall` defined in `ubuntu.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `allpkgs=aptitude search -F "%p" --disable-columns ~i` in `debian.plugin.zsh`.
20. Alias `mydeb=time dpkg-buildpackage -rfakeroot -us -uc` defined in `ubuntu.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `mydeb=time dpkg-buildpackage -rfakeroot -us -uc` in `debian.plugin.zsh`.
21. Alias `mis=microk8s.istio` defined in `microk8s.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `mis=meteor install-sdk` in `meteor.plugin.zsh`.
22. Alias `jh=juju help` defined in `juju.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `jh=jhbuild` in `jhbuild.plugin.zsh`.
23. Alias `zcl=sudo zypper cl` defined in `suse.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `zcl=sudo zypper clean` in `suse.plugin.zsh`.
24. Alias `h=history` defined in `history.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `h=history` in `common-aliases.plugin.zsh`.
25. Alias `rn=react-native` defined in `react-native.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `rn=rails notes` in `rails.plugin.zsh`.
26. Alias `dr=docker container run` defined in `docker.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `dr=dotnet run` in `dotnet.plugin.zsh`.
27. Alias `rake=noglob rake` defined in `rake.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `rake=_rake_command` in `rails.plugin.zsh`.
28. Alias `github=frontend github` defined in `frontend-search.plugin.zsh` already exists as alias `github=web_search github` in `web-search.plugin.zsh`.
29. Alias `stackoverflow=frontend stackoverflow` defined in `frontend-search.plugin.zsh
` already exists as alias `stackoverflow=web_search stackoverflow` in `web-search.plugin.zsh`.

body:
Some things I spotted along the way:
- We're not really interested in the value itself of the alias, I'd only save the file where is declared and nothing else. My arch for it would be (automatically supporting >2 repeated aliases, which now is an issue:
```json
[
  {
    "alias": "<duplicated>"
	"locations": ["<file1>", "<file2>", "...", "<fileN>"]
  }
]
```
  Main issue with it: if we have to equal aliases in the same file, weird corner cases might appear.
- For the moment we're only matching `alias foo='bar'`, and aliases might be declared without quotes, etc.
- We only match aliases defined in the start of the line, let's remove the `^`.

author: romkatv

body:
> I'm suggesting this:
> 
> ```diff
> - print -n "\nusing local history"
> + zle -M 'using local history'
> ```
> 
> Run `man zshzle` and search for `zle -M` for docs.

This is exactly what I tried at first until you mentioned `zle -I` as an alternative. I don't remember what the problem with this was, but back then I opted for the other solution for some reason. I will revisit and check this later.
author: Saduff

body:
Both `zle -M` and `zle -I` are valid ways to print something from a zle widget. The former makes the output a part of zle itself while the latter forcefully abandons the current prompt. Switching between local and global history is like switching between viins and vicmd. The latter does not leave a prompt behind, so it makes sense for the former to also not do that.

Regardless of what you decide to do, I think the most important thing is to make this behavior optional. The best indicator would be within the prompt itself (just like for viins/vicmd). Let people do that if they want and let them disable the message.
author: romkatv

body:
> I don't remember what the problem with this was, but back then I opted for the other solution for some reason. I will revisit and check this later.

Thanks @Saduff, that would be awesome. Because so far in this discussion the motivation for removing those lines remains unclear.

> Switching between local and global history is like switching between viins and vicmd. The latter does not leave a prompt behind, so it makes sense for the former to also not do that.

Great analogy @romkatv. By "leave the prompt behind", do you mean basically act as if a command was actually submitted and regenerate the prompt a line or two down? If so, then I agree it would be most natural to switch history modes without leaving the current prompt. That behavior always bugged me about this plugin.

> the most important thing is to make this behavior optional. The best indicator would be within the prompt itself (just like for viins/vicmd). Let people do that if they want and let them disable the message.

+100000 for toggle-ability. These messages fix an issue from an earlier state of the plugin where there was no visual indication of which mode you were in. But now that we can indicate it via the P10K prompt (http://romkatv/powerlevel10k#2384), printing the message doesn't actually add value (for P10K users at least)
author: cohml

body: Indeed, powerlevel10k shows the global/local history indicator by default for users of this plugin. This was added within https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k/issues/2384#issuecomment-1649649782. Other themes can do the same thing. It would be *splendid* if one could disable the printing from per-directory-history when switching mode. When printing is enabled, `zle -M` is the least intrusive way to achieve it.
author: romkatv

> It would be _splendid_ if one could disable the printing from per-directory-history when switching mode.

Okay, I checked it again and refreshed my memory. I'm pretty sure I chose `zle -I` instead of `zle -M` to preserve the way it worked originally with the print statements, just without breaking the prompt. I personally didn't see any issue with the prompt being regenerated as shown in #12068 as that's how it worked prior to my commit.

body:
> Indeed, powerlevel10k shows the global/local history indicator by default for users of this plugin.

@romkatv this is great! I've noticed, however that the local/global indicator in the prompt segment does not update when toggling the state. Only when I enter a command and re-render the prompt does it display the correct state.  Is this normal, or have I misconfigured my prompt?  Is there a way to make this update the segment in real time?
author: mikegreiling

body:
If anybody else lands here with the same issue, I ended up resolving this with the following:

```zsh
# Ensure the p10k prompt gets refreshed when toggling local/global history
# see https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k/issues/72#issuecomment-1008262525
function toggle-per-directory-history-and-redraw-prompt {
  per-directory-history-toggle-history
  zle push-line
  zle accept-line
}

zle -N toggle-per-directory-history-and-redraw-prompt
bindkey ${PER_DIRECTORY_HISTORY_TOGGLE:-'^G'} toggle-per-directory-history-and-redraw-prompt
```

It would be nice if this could be done in-place rather than acting as though I just ran a blank command, but it gets the job done.

body: Can you post the output of `oc completion zsh` under these versions? I fear your `sed` command might be too general, so I'd like to hardcode the full line regex.
author: mcornella

body:
Sure! This is the output from `oc completion zsh` command

```zsh
#compdef kubectl
compdef _kubectl kubectl

#compdef _oc oc

# zsh completion for oc                                   -*- shell-script -*-

__oc_debug()
{
    local file="$BASH_COMP_DEBUG_FILE"
    if [[ -n ${file} ]]; then
        echo "$*" >> "${file}"
    fi
}

_oc()
{
    local shellCompDirectiveError=1
    local shellCompDirectiveNoSpace=2
    local shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp=4
    local shellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt=8
    local shellCompDirectiveFilterDirs=16

    local lastParam lastChar flagPrefix requestComp out directive comp lastComp noSpace
    local -a completions

    __oc_debug "\n========= starting completion logic =========="
    __oc_debug "CURRENT: ${CURRENT}, words[*]: ${words[*]}"

    # The user could have moved the cursor backwards on the command-line.
    # We need to trigger completion from the $CURRENT location, so we need
    # to truncate the command-line ($words) up to the $CURRENT location.
    # (We cannot use $CURSOR as its value does not work when a command is an alias.)
    words=("${=words[1,CURRENT]}")
    __oc_debug "Truncated words[*]: ${words[*]},"

    lastParam=${words[-1]}
    lastChar=${lastParam[-1]}
    __oc_debug "lastParam: ${lastParam}, lastChar: ${lastChar}"

    # For zsh, when completing a flag with an = (e.g., oc -n=<TAB>)
    # completions must be prefixed with the flag
    setopt local_options BASH_REMATCH
    if [[ "${lastParam}" =~ '-.*=' ]]; then
        # We are dealing with a flag with an =
        flagPrefix="-P ${BASH_REMATCH}"
    fi

    # Prepare the command to obtain completions
    requestComp="${words[1]} __complete ${words[2,-1]}"
    if [ "${lastChar}" = "" ]; then
        # If the last parameter is complete (there is a space following it)
        # We add an extra empty parameter so we can indicate this to the go completion code.
        __oc_debug "Adding extra empty parameter"
        requestComp="${requestComp} \"\""
    fi

    __oc_debug "About to call: eval ${requestComp}"

    # Use eval to handle any environment variables and such
    out=$(eval ${requestComp} 2>/dev/null)
    __oc_debug "completion output: ${out}"

    # Extract the directive integer following a : from the last line
    local lastLine
    while IFS='\n' read -r line; do
        lastLine=${line}
    done < <(printf "%s\n" "${out[@]}")
    __oc_debug "last line: ${lastLine}"

    if [ "${lastLine[1]}" = : ]; then
        directive=${lastLine[2,-1]}
        # Remove the directive including the : and the newline
        local suffix
        (( suffix=${#lastLine}+2))
        out=${out[1,-$suffix]}
    else
        # There is no directive specified.  Leave $out as is.
        __oc_debug "No directive found.  Setting do default"
        directive=0
    fi

    __oc_debug "directive: ${directive}"
    __oc_debug "completions: ${out}"
    __oc_debug "flagPrefix: ${flagPrefix}"

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveError)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __oc_debug "Completion received error. Ignoring completions."
        return
    fi

    while IFS='\n' read -r comp; do
        if [ -n "$comp" ]; then
            # If requested, completions are returned with a description.
            # The description is preceded by a TAB character.
            # For zsh's _describe, we need to use a : instead of a TAB.
            # We first need to escape any : as part of the completion itself.
            comp=${comp//:/\\:}

            local tab=$(printf '\t')
            comp=${comp//$tab/:}

            __oc_debug "Adding completion: ${comp}"
            completions+=${comp}
            lastComp=$comp
        fi
    done < <(printf "%s\n" "${out[@]}")

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoSpace)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __oc_debug "Activating nospace."
        noSpace="-S ''"
    fi

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt)) -ne 0 ]; then
        # File extension filtering
        local filteringCmd
        filteringCmd='_files'
        for filter in ${completions[@]}; do
            if [ ${filter[1]} != '*' ]; then
                # zsh requires a glob pattern to do file filtering
                filter="\*.$filter"
            fi
            filteringCmd+=" -g $filter"
        done
        filteringCmd+=" ${flagPrefix}"

        __oc_debug "File filtering command: $filteringCmd"
        _arguments '*:filename:'"$filteringCmd"
    elif [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveFilterDirs)) -ne 0 ]; then
        # File completion for directories only
        local subDir
        subdir="${completions[1]}"
        if [ -n "$subdir" ]; then
            __oc_debug "Listing directories in $subdir"
            pushd "${subdir}" >/dev/null 2>&1
        else
            __oc_debug "Listing directories in ."
        fi

        local result
        _arguments '*:dirname:_files -/'" ${flagPrefix}"
        result=$?
        if [ -n "$subdir" ]; then
            popd >/dev/null 2>&1
        fi
        return $result
    else
        __oc_debug "Calling _describe"
        if eval _describe "completions" completions $flagPrefix $noSpace; then
            __oc_debug "_describe found some completions"

            # Return the success of having called _describe
            return 0
        else
            __oc_debug "_describe did not find completions."
            __oc_debug "Checking if we should do file completion."
            if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp)) -ne 0 ]; then
                __oc_debug "deactivating file completion"

                # We must return an error code here to let zsh know that there were no
                # completions found by _describe; this is what will trigger other
                # matching algorithms to attempt to find completions.
                # For example zsh can match letters in the middle of words.
                return 1
            else
                # Perform file completion
                __oc_debug "Activating file completion"

                # We must return the result of this command, so it must be the
                # last command, or else we must store its result to return it.
                _arguments '*:filename:_files'" ${flagPrefix}"
            fi
        fi
    fi
}

# don't run the completion function when being source-ed or eval-ed
if [ "$funcstack[1]" = "_oc" ]; then
	_oc
fi
```

body:
I haven't found this to solve the issue with `xclip`. The terminal tab still hangs for a bit when closing it and then the xclip process keeps alive without an attached tty:

![Captura de pantalla de 2022-06-12 17-23-54](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1441704/173240339-e20274ff-d4d3-4b37-bb40-26b0f200f28d.png)

_In the comment it should say pts/2_
![Captura de pantalla de 2022-06-12 17-24-34](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1441704/173240342-595a83ec-e260-492b-b1eb-3843ab9f10e7.png)

BTW, even though the screenshot shows the function using `nohup`, the same happens using `&|`.

I've even tried [running it with `setsid`](https://superuser.com/a/1430883/545959) so that it does not have a tty, but the problem is not that, it's that it is a child process of the terminal emulator (in my case, `gnome-terminal-server`):

![Captura de pantalla de 2022-06-12 18-07-30](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1441704/173242414-b0b0d154-ed22-4ddf-b4aa-a9b41eea6949.png)

It seems like the best workaround is to prefer `xsel` over `xclip`: https://github.com/kovidgoyal/kitty/issues/828.

----

As per `wl-copy`, I haven't had the time to test it out yet.
author: mcornella

body:
For `wl-copy`, in my tests (Fedora 35 Silverblue) it looked like the only needed change was to silence output with `&>/dev/null`, but using `&|` as well doesn't cause any issues.

```sh
function clipcopy() { wl-copy < "${1:-/dev/stdin}" &>/dev/null &|; }
```

The extra cat was not needed in my tests, but please correct me and provide a reproduction case where that is crucial for the fix to work.
author: mcornella

body:
@mcornella Thank you for such a comprehansive reply!

As for `xclip`, it is really Kitty's oddity (or a feature) to have such a deafult setting. Gnome Terminal, XFCE Terminal and others do actually close after small pause. Not sure about what is better: to do some overkill (but hopefully working everywhere) as I suggested or to give up on such uncommon behavior like Kitty's (which can be [overrided via settings](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/conf/#opt-kitty.close_on_child_death) though).

As for `function clipcopy() { wl-copy < "${1:-/dev/stdin}" &>/dev/null &|; }` — tested it with the both Kitty and Gnome Terminal by just invoking it, and it does not even try to input anything from TTY in my case. But when I do `echo 123 | clipcopy`, it does its work nicely. That's why I used `cat` explicitly. Not sure why, but this helped...

So I'll be happy to improve my PR, but there is still no confidence if it will really benefit from the both changes =).
author: dluciv

body:
> As for `function clipcopy() { wl-copy < "${1:-/dev/stdin}" &>/dev/null &|; }` — tested it with the both Kitty and Gnome Terminal by just invoking it, and it does not even try to input anything from TTY in my case. But when I do `echo 123 | clipcopy`

Oh, that was supposed to be the only way to be used, but it does seem like an improvement to also allow waiting for input from the user to send to the clipboard. Many other clipboard methods do not allow this as well, so I think I'll separate both changes into different commits.

body:
Actually the main problem in omz are PRs that just add aliases. We have a lot of aliases in general, and also in the plugins and this should be limited in some way. We have discussed with Marc that probably the best option is to ask not only which alias you want to add but also which is the workflow that needs that alias. I'll make an example.
Imagine someone propose an alias that is `alias gsa=git submodule add`. How many times per year a regular user adds a submodule in git? Roughly, once a month maximum. Just for once a month, you can type `g submodule add` and everything is going to be OK. In case you are a hard user of that alias, just add it in your zshrc. The opposite option is `git pull`. A regular user probably execute a git pull more than once a day, in this case an alias gives real value to users.
Before doing this of mark as stale, we should close all PRs adding aliases asking for this new field in the template, to see if that alias actually help users or it's just one more.
If we do this we'll probably close more that 200 PRs, and then we can focus in those that are actually interesting for the development of OMZ.
What do you think about?
author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala in my opinion, the issue isn't that the PRs are for simple aliases.  It is that there are not enough maintainers to knock the easy ones out.  I've started reviewing PRs here to help but I don't have merge capability.  Does adding a simple alias to the plugin cause anyone issue? Not really, but I understand that affects all subsequent PRs that are doing a "simple alias" also.  They need to merge or rebase to continue.  That can be done with a button push on the GitHub UI now.  Do you think gatekeeping which aliases are "more used" than just merging PRs might be a heavier lift for maintainers?  🤷 

You have much more experience with this project's interaction and I yield to your better judgment on this.
author: jmeridth

body: Yes, I see your point, but there are some people (several actually) complaining about omz, and saying that we have a lot of aliases added that cannot be disabled, and I absolutely agree (and I think Marc also) with them. That's why we are not merging everything that appears (there's an issue related, #10644)
author: carlosala

body: @carlosala That definitely makes sense and is a perspective I hadn't thought of.  Thank you for sharing that context.  I'm going to close this PR.  Thank you for your patience, time and being open for discussion.  Cheers.
author: jmeridth

body: Thanks also for your contribution too, I really appreciate that! 😉

author: jeesang7

body:
You can enable [`fetch.prune`](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-fetch#Documentation/git-fetch.txt-fetchprune) in your git configuration to make both `git fetch` and `git pull` act as if `--prune` was always passed:

```
$ git config --global fetch.prune true
```

Since this can be done with config for *all* commands, I don't think we need an extra alias. Close @carlosala ?

body: I would like an explanation of the reasoning on how it would handle the actual authorization process? Is it 100% token based and will they ever expire?
author: larson-carter

body:
Authorization is done completely via the sso flow:
- if token is not valid anymore (checked by doing a simple call), it will request a new token. Normally this involves displaying a URL you have to visit and enter some code, which in most cases can be opened automatically in a browser. As a user you still have to confirm the request
- if token is valid at the time of change, it will continue to use it

if the token becomes invalid/expired, one has to either do `aws sso login` or "switch" to the same profile. Expiration is tied to the sso settings within aws. (in my cases, sessions get expired)

I do not think this change should exist. If you want to install Oh My Zsh unattended, please use this command: `sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)" "" --unattended`.

Generally, changing the default shell to zsh requires user interaction to enter your password

If you're running the Oh My Zsh install script as part of an automated install, you can pass the --unattended flag to the install.sh > script. This will have the effect of not trying to change the default shell, and it also won't run zsh when the installation has finished.

body:
It's been discussed already in previous PRs that if someone's using multiple AUR helpers for some reason, then they'd be better invoking them directly or manually overriding aliases instead of relying on plugins.

IMHO ordering by popularity is not the right approach. Popularity is practically unmeasurable in the absence of consistent telemetry, and I don't believe that number of GitHub stars reflects anything at all. Besides popularity may change over time, and we don't want to shuffle the order of plugins in this list (and once again breaking the expected behavior for those who have multiple AUR helpers and still use aliases!). So, overall it's a "no" from me, sorry.

body:
Haven't dissected what's happening in this script, but since this commit/PR I see:

```
fzf_setup_using_debian:source:40: no such file or directory: /usr/share/doc/fzf/examples/key-bindings.zsh
```

And consequently FZF doesn't work on <kbd>ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>r</kbd> (and other hotkeys).

Arch package has the following contents:

```console
$ pacman -Ql fzf
fzf /usr/
fzf /usr/bin/
fzf /usr/bin/fzf
fzf /usr/bin/fzf-tmux
fzf /usr/share/
fzf /usr/share/fish/
fzf /usr/share/fish/vendor_functions.d/
fzf /usr/share/fish/vendor_functions.d/fzf_key_bindings.fish
fzf /usr/share/fzf/
fzf /usr/share/fzf/completion.bash
fzf /usr/share/fzf/completion.zsh
fzf /usr/share/fzf/key-bindings.bash
fzf /usr/share/fzf/key-bindings.zsh
fzf /usr/share/licenses/
fzf /usr/share/licenses/fzf/
fzf /usr/share/licenses/fzf/LICENSE
fzf /usr/share/man/
fzf /usr/share/man/man1/
fzf /usr/share/man/man1/fzf-tmux.1.gz
fzf /usr/share/man/man1/fzf.1.gz
fzf /usr/share/vim/
fzf /usr/share/vim/vimfiles/
fzf /usr/share/vim/vimfiles/doc/
fzf /usr/share/vim/vimfiles/doc/fzf.txt
fzf /usr/share/vim/vimfiles/plugin/
fzf /usr/share/vim/vimfiles/plugin/fzf.vim
```

---

EDIT: for me `dpkg` is installed, and so `|| ! dpkg -s fzf &>/dev/null` would have returned true (exit code 0) previously, resulting in an immediate `return 1` out of `fzf_setup_using_debian()`.

body:
The function also supports termux which uses `pkg`, but I see I have both `apt` and `apt-get` installed.

Some Debian-based systems have one or the other, so we should check for either of them:

```sh
(( ${+commands[apt]} || ${+commands[apt-get]} )) 
```
author: mcornella

body:
How about we do this?
```bash
  if ((( ! $+commands[apt] )) && (( ! $+commands[apt-get] ))) || (( ! $+commands[dpkg] )); then
    # Not a debian based distro
    return 1
  fi

# ...
  case $PREFIX in
    *com.termux*)
# ...
      ;;
    *)
      if [[ ! -f /usr/bin/fzf ]] || [[ ! -d /usr/share/doc/fzf/examples ]]; then
        # fzf or the completions are not installed
        return 1
      fi
```

The idea is just to be very conservative about checks: as long as it doesn't look like a debian (missing any command, or missing file at the right place) we `return 1`.
author: ppwwyyxx

body: I'm concerned if that file will belong to every apt-like distro. Do you have apt installed? It seems like the definitive solution
author: carlosala

body:
```
if ((( ! $+commands[apt] )) && (( ! $+commands[apt-get] ))) || (( ! $+commands[dpkg] )); then
    # Not a debian based distro
    return 1
  fi
```

This logic will not match on a system that has dpkg installed but not any of the others, which is the current issue. This would still resort to checking the package files, which is very brittle.

body:
I was frustrated by the lack of auto completions too and came upon this solution which updates both the _comps and fpath variable.

```bash
if [[ (! -v "_comps[brew]") && $+commands[brew] ]] ; then
  # Add all available Homebrew completion files if they aren't already
  BREW_COMPLETIONS="$(brew --prefix)/share/zsh/site-functions"
  if [[ -d $BREW_COMPLETIONS ]]; then
    fpath+=$BREW_COMPLETIONS

    typeset -g -A _comps

    for file in $BREW_COMPLETIONS/*(N)
    do
      comp=$file:t
      autoload -Uz $comp
      _comps[${comp:1}]=$comp
      unset comp
    done
  fi

  unset BREW_COMPLETIONS
fi
```

author: Nadhum

body:
That's because it caches the aliases [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/7dcabbe6826073ef6069c8a4b6f9a943f00d2df0/lib/grep.zsh#L32) and [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/7dcabbe6826073ef6069c8a4b6f9a943f00d2df0/lib/grep.zsh#L5) tries to load them instead of redefining. If you remove `$ZSH/cache/grep-alias` it'll work.
I would directly remove that caching of aliases, I think it's totally useless, do you @mcornella know why are them there?

body: I had the same experience as @elisiariocouto. Besides, in my understanding, this patch is completely unnecessary with regard to the original issue. The regex used to extract the profiles names had already been fixed to make the `profile` prefix optional.
author: paulolieuthier

body:
> this patch is completely unnecessary

AWS CLI profiles can be defined in `AWS_SHARED_CREDENTIALS_FILE` (`~/.aws/credentials`) as well.
The previous code did not show profiles defined in this file.

Using AWS logic to process the profiles makes the code more forward-compatible to any change AWS will ever do to profiles.

With that said - I understand that Python bootstrap might be slow in some cases, so I suggest we'll change the old regex-based profile extraction to also look at the other file, and allow opting-out the new way (or maybe remove it completely).

Any thoughts?
author: noamcohen97

body:
I agree with you that using AWS' own tooling would be ideal and future-proof. But speed-wise, when having multiple profiles, I think it defeats the purpose of using the plugin.

I believe the regex-based solution is good enough and should be improved to support the configuration environment variable. From what I read in the documentation, the file to be read is not the credentials file, but the config file. And so `AWS_CONFIG_FILE` should be used instead of `AWS_SHARED_CREDENTIALS_FILE`. Is there a scenario in which the config file is not available?

Another thing that would make the command faster is to get the profile region from the config file as well, instead of running `aws configure get region`.
author: paulolieuthier

body:
> From what I read in the documentation, the file to be read is not the credentials file, but the config file

Both files are used when searching for a profile:
https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/guide/credentials.html#configuring-credentials

author: MrAureliusR

body:
As explain in [here](https://www.gnu.org/software/gawk/manual/html_node/Bracket-Expressions.html), space characters matched by `\s` are space, TAB, newline, carriage return, formfeed and vertical tab.

Of these, only space and TAB make sense to be matched by awk, as newline and carriage return are the line separators, and formfeed/vertical tab are never present in the wild.

People used to alias rm to rm -I

author: rwmitchell

body:
Thanks for reviewing!

Personally I've been aliasing `rm` to something like...

```zsh
alias rm="echo 'Think again and type: command rm'"
```

... ever since I had an unpleasant experience of executing `rm -rf $SOMETHING/` while forgetting to set variable `$SOMETHING`, and ended up executing `rm -rf /`. With the aliased `rm`, the above would instead print this out, with variables and wildcards properly evaluated:

```plaintext
Think again and type: command rm -rf /
```

So I can see clearly what's going on.

While this reason may not be justifiable enough, it has not brought me much trouble thus far, because aliases are not evaluated by shell scripts by default (but not if they are `source`d, like the npm plugin). There are other `rm` invocations in my OMZ, but they are unshadowed with `command rm` ([example](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/570158e464c9f57ab03c4162b4e6853b2c7c650d/oh-my-zsh.sh#L117)). So I believe it's justifiable to bring this to the npm plugin as well.

We're currently polling on whether to proceed with this change on [this Discord message](https://discord.com/channels/642496866407284746/829052424723365889/1158467038537334836), in case you want to get your voice heard.

> Do we really need --rebase aliases anyway? One can configure pull.rebase = true and it will be used by default. I imagine most people using these aliases would be better off making it the default.

I agree with you that a config option is best for your use case if you do this by default. body:
Hi! All this plugins (like `gh`, `deno`, and many others) are built in the same way that doesn't block execution of the shell and doesn't affect the performance.
Moreover, most of this calls are implemented by a famous rust library, and take less than 0.5ms.
Changing one would mean losing all generalisation and, in my opinion, is worst than removing some unneeded executions that doesn't affect performance.
Thanks for your contribution!
author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala 

> Hi! All this plugins (like `gh`, `deno`, and many others) are built in the same way that doesn't block execution of the shell and doesn't affect the performance. Moreover, most of this calls are implemented by a famous rust library

Well, I guess you're right about this if it comes to rustup and even more so cargo (the file isn't even generated in that case).

> and take less than 0.5ms

Which is quite a bit, when cumulated with multiple plugins.

However:
I'm quite sure that what you say is not true for gh and especially not for bitwarden #11181 (it is a JavaScript application).
I had these caching changes active on my computers, and I claim I felt a difference in shell start up time, if I have it active.

Anyway:
I won't argue with you about this PR or even less about #11230 - I can and will totally accept you don't want this merged, and I appreciate your maintenance efforts (that includes denial of contributions in some cases as well). I know and respect that.

But the fact that I will keep the alternations on my personal setup, despite the effort it takes for me, to rebase with origin/master every now and then, might point out to you, that there could still be some relevance to this for some.

body:
I think there's an issue with this PR.  In my environment, I get:

```
me$ zsh
/path/to/oh-my-zsh/plugins/wd/wd.sh:399: parse error near `]]'
/path/to/oh-my-zsh/plugins/wd/wd.sh:399: parse error near `]]'
/path/to/oh-my-zsh/plugins/wd/wd.sh:399: parse error near `]]'
/path/to/oh-my-zsh/plugins/wd/wd.sh:399: parse error near `]]'
/path/to/oh-my-zsh/plugins/wd/wd.sh:399: parse error near `]]'
```

when I go to launch zsh from bash.

The following patch fixes it:

```diff
diff --git a/plugins/wd/wd.sh b/plugins/wd/wd.sh
index e51cf906..e38f663f 100644
--- a/plugins/wd/wd.sh
+++ b/plugins/wd/wd.sh
@@ -396,7 +396,7 @@ fi
 # disable extendedglob for the complete wd execution time
 setopt | grep -q extendedglob
 wd_extglob_is_set=$?
-[[ $wd_extglob_is_set ]] && setopt noextendedglob
+[[ -z $wd_extglob_is_set ]] && setopt noextendedglob
 
 # load warp points
 typeset -A points
@@ -484,7 +484,7 @@ fi
 # if not, next time warp will pick up variables from this run
 # remember, there's no sub shell
 
-[[ $wd_extglob_is_set ]] && setopt extendedglob
+[[ -z $wd_extglob_is_set ]] && setopt extendedglob
 
 unset wd_extglob_is_set
 unset wd_warp
```

I presume I got the `-z` right, since grep will exit 0 when it finds a match (which you added in the diff on this PR).

@carlosala @alpha-tango-kilo

body:
Sure:

```shell
❯ which z
z () {
	[ $# -gt 0 ] && _z "$*" && return
	cd "$(_z -l 2>&1 | fzf --height 40% --nth 2.. --reverse --inline-info +s --tac --query "${*##-* }" | sed 's/^[0-9,.]* *//')"
}
```

My dude.

You made exactly 5 PRs and no more on October 7, on a repository with the Hacktoberfest label.

```
This plugin adds smart tab completion for [TaskWarrior](https://taskwarrior.org/), a tool for for managing your *to-do's* from the command line.

You can:

  - Recommended: Change ~/.zshrc so that it does not perform console I/O
    after the instant prompt preamble. See the link below for details.

    * You will not see this error message again.
    * Zsh will start quickly and prompt will update smoothly.

  - Suppress this warning either by running p10k configure or by manually
    defining the following parameter:

      typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_INSTANT_PROMPT=quiet

    * You will not see this error message again.
    * Zsh will start quickly but prompt will jump down after initialization.

  - Disable instant prompt either by running p10k configure or by manually
    defining the following parameter:

      typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_INSTANT_PROMPT=off

    * You will not see this error message again.
    * Zsh will start slowly.

  - Do nothing.

    * You will see this error message every time you start zsh.
    * Zsh will start quickly but prompt will jump down after initialization.

For details, see:
https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k/blob/master/README.md#instant-prompt

-- console output produced during zsh initialization follows --

~/dotfiles master !7
❯

```

body:
File creations (with touch) are not atomic, there could be two processes finding no lock file and enter the if at the same time before the `touch` runs.

See the `check_for_upgrade.sh` script for an example of a lock mechanism using `mkdir`.

There is also a lock functionality in a module, look for `zsystem flock` in https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Zsh-Modules.html.

body:
Hi Tommy, thank you for the contribution.

When reviewing new plugins, we have to consider that there is a big number of plugins already included by default within OMZ.

In this case, I can see that it might be useful for your use case, however this might be less useful for the workflow of other people for the following reasons:
1. When writing a script filename, unless you use the prefix `./` you won't get autocompletion of the filename, which already will tell you that you're missing the prefix.
2. If you add the current directory to your `$PATH`, it is then not necessary to add the prefix. I consider that more useful, which is a single line of code in my `.zshrc` file:
   ```sh
   path+=(.)
   ```

That said, I'd like to thank you for the submission, and wish to see what you come up with next. Cheers!

body:
Using the man page for lsof I found the following command that will automatically select only zsh processes and print the directories on each line, which works for directories with spaces:
```
lsof -d cwd -c zsh -a -w -Fn | sed -n 's/^n//p'
```

Pending to check if it works for macOS lsof.
author: mcornella

body:
Your change works on my system:

```darwin*)  dirs=( $( lsof -d cwd -c zsh -a -w -Fn | sed -n 's/^n//p' ) ) ;;```

It does pick up '/' as an option, but I don't think it is worth filtering out.

body:
So, I tried. It was quite tricky to get the source code sorted alphabetically by command as I did in the readme. I'm trying to do this with vim-fu because doing this by hand is outside the oberton window of tedium for me, personally.

With the readme it was easy, because I could visual-select to reorder the whole columns in the table, then `:sort`, then flip the columns back into the right order.

I tried to do a similar thing with the source, but there are functions in there, there are distinct sections related to certain functionality where the ideal sort order is unclear, etc.

As an alternative, I'd suggest sorting each chunk of aliases in the source alphabetically, rather than try to have the source order match the documentation order exactly 1:1. What do you think?

author: mcornella

body:
As a minimal example, I have a commit graph like this:

```
* e97c34b (HEAD -> main) Update amend
| * f229734 (origin/main-tmp) Update
|/  
* 7aaf892 (origin/main, origin/HEAD) Initial commit
```

Then when I `gpf origin main:main-tmp` or `gpf origin HEAD:main-tmp` (this is what I mostly do), it warns me as expected:

```
To github.com:aekt/gpf.git
 ! [rejected]        main -> main-tmp (remote ref updated since checkout)
error: failed to push some refs to 'github.com:aekt/gpf.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the tip of the remote-tracking
hint: branch has been updated since the last checkout. You may want
hint: to integrate those changes locally (e.g., 'git pull ...')
hint: before forcing an update.
```

Aliases should not be created in lib for utilities that are not routinely included in a default OS install.  If everything is open game, even if it is preceded by a test to see if the app is installed, where does it end?
Each test takes a bit of time, not universally necessary.  OMZ already supports plugins that allows people to optionally enable settings they want, why not use it?

author: ohmyzsh

body:
Hi, thanks for the PR!

Could you add some detail as to the reasons for this change and the potential effects (good and bad)?

I might be missing something, but there are a couple reasons I'm unsure about merging this:

1. I'm confused as to the benefit. Wouldn't you always want to run these scripts with the locally installed version of react-native? When I have a react native project, I'll need to run `npm install` or `yarn install`, so I'll have react-native available locally, which means running `npx` is just going to use the local version of `react-native` cli for the project, right? If it's not available locally, I haven't installed my project's node modules correctly, which means running these scripts with the latest version available on the npm registry is going to fail (best case), no?

2. It's opinionated about which package manager should be used. (Plenty of people are using `yarn` instead of `npm` or `npx`; running without `npx` defaults users to whichever package manager they used to install `react-native`.) If I called `npx react-native run`, and I have used `yarn` for managing packages and executing scripts in my project, will this still respect that or will it execute with `npx`? Fwiw, the CLI project is [not opinionated about which package manager to use](https://github.com/react-native-community/cli#usage-in-an-existing-react-native-project). That leads me to not want to break that chain of being open about which package manager to use for these cli scripts.
author: esthor

body:
The `acs --help` output here is misleading. The `filter` positional argument should just be given with `filter`, as in `acs a`, `acs git commit`, etc.

I've pushed a fix that should make the help output clearer:

```
usage: acs [-h] [-g GROUP_LIST] [--groups] [<keyword> [<keyword> ...]]

Pretty print aliases.

positional arguments:
  <keyword>             search aliases matching keywords

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -g GROUP_LIST, --group GROUP_LIST
                        only print aliases in given groups
  --groups              only print alias groups
```

body:
OK it looks like the same happens with `aliases` and the rest of special parameters when we're doing `array=()` (empty array).

When that happens, the resulting hash table does not actually get modified, I don't know exactly why by looking at the code: https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh/blob/c006d760/Src/Modules/parameter.c#L1767-L1807

I have pushed a version that uses `unalias` directly in a "smarter" way to avoid many calls, but I don't know if it is actually slower. Could you try it out with `zsh-bench` again?

----

EDIT: it looks like the culprit is that the hashtable is actually empty, so this check matches and nothing is done: https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh/blob/c006d7609703afcfb2162c36d4f745125df45879/Src/Modules/parameter.c#L1773-L1774
author: mcornella

Sweet, no worries. I've copied that code over to the changelog file.

I ended up not adding a check for jetbrains because the check I originally took from symfony assumed _everything_ supports hyperlinks, and only marked otherwise if something explicitly doesn't support it - but the checks you have at the moment assume the opposite - so jetbrains should end up returning `1` anyway.

Let me know if you need any more changes once you've gotten a chance to do some testing.

@carlosala are you recommending a change or just pointing out more duplicate code?

If the former, can you please be very specific? Do you recommend I copy/paste the relevant code or is there a way to share it between the two?

I don't think I'll be able to use that existing function for the formatting in `sed`, and it seems like bad practice to copy a function to format the links if I'm only using it once in this file.

Also are you saying that the fallback for this text if hyperlinks aren't supported should be to use underlines? That would be a departure from the current formatting.

I do notice the syntax for formatting the link is slightly different in that function though so when I'm on my pc again tomorrow I'll swap out so the same formatting code is used across the board.

author: carlosala

body:
Hmm okay so I tried putting the formatting (i.e. `printf '\033]8;;%s\033\\%s\033]8;;\033\\' "$url" "$text"'`) into this PR but it resulted in incorrect results, e.g:
> - [e\[33m95d0c4b](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/95d0c4b) [theme-and-appearance]

So for whatever reason it looks like the formatting used in `upgrade.sh` isn't suitable here. My guessing is it has to do with the way the colour is formatted vs how the hyperlink is formatted but bash isn't really my wheelhouse so I'm not sure what to do about it (or if anything even needs to be done about it).

If someone knows what's going on and can suggest improvements I'll be happy to make them - but I'll leave it as is for now, since this works well and I can't find a way to integrate the code from `upgrade.sh` without breaking things.

Those are global aliases, that means that are not only commands. For example, `mv foo ...` would actually expand to `mv foo ../..`.
That's why we can't accept your proposal. ~In that case, I don't see why you have issues with the new system, you can just create the aliases and omz will keep them for you.~

EDIT: I see the regression, if there was an alias defined with the same name and omz overwrites it, our function will not be smart enough to detect it. We'll work to fix it.
author: carlosala

body:
Ah neat, I didn't realize global aliases worked that way. That's actually quite convenient.

Is it expected that `...`, which expands to `../..`, would actually traverse up the directory tree? How does `../..` do that without a `cd`? Is that a zsh thing, an OMZ thing, or just a system shortcut?
author: mattdodge

body: It's a zsh option, that is set up [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/2e7a247cab663fbc0aafdefa15bf10eb4dd64563/lib/directories.zsh#L2) in ohmyzsh. It is disabled by default.

body:
@carlosala I'm still having issues, even on that branch. Although it seems like my root problem is something with `auto_cd` getting permission denied errors. I get the error when I run `/`, `../..`, or even `~`. All of those directories have correct chmod/chown permissions too. Weird.

To at least test your PR, I've overridden the `...` alias and set `zstyle ':omz:lib:directories' aliases no` - and at least that is respecting my alias. So I think the work you've done there is good - thanks for that! Sounds like I just have something else wrong with my `auto_cd` setup and this recent change is just revealing it.

body:
> What do the alias letters indicate?
> 
> I added them locally as glgb and glgba. Already had glg for log output, added b to indicate branch log.
> 
> that’s the problem with most aliases, the names only make sense to the author.

The letters was used to indicate glc (git log compact), the "a" is added to get the commit-hash and author info as well. The workflow would be to investigate the reposirory using glc, identifying dangling branches etc. If actuall work is being needed, the user would likely have to run glca.

Although it wasn't my intention in making this change, this PR may also help to address the issues encountered in the discussion at #11602.

However, to _completely_ address that issue, it may also be advisable to **unset** the `PATH` envvar in the `env` command (`/usr/bin/env -u PATH ZSH="$ZSH"`), so that it will reset to the system default and allow `sh "$ZSH/tools/uninstall.sh"` to run successfully even if the `$PATH` was previously broken.

Thoughts on adding a `-u PATH` to the command?

body:
@sdondley docker folks are switching to cobra v2 completions, generated by `docker completion zsh`. As it's new, it'll keep improving more and more. For example, it allows to have completion for `docker compose`, `docker buildx`, and other CLI plugins.
If you want to have another completion, you can of course download any script you want and add it to a directory present in `$fpath`.
author: carlosala

body: OK, but if I have an older version of docker on the machine, this new version of the plugin won't work at all and even if I have a newer version, it will not work at all for many subcommands. Seems to me you should wait until it's at least as good as the old version before using `docker completion zsh` don't you think?
author: sdondley

body: @sdondley I see your point, but the new version has that CLI plugins completion that the old doesn't, and the old one hasn't been updated for 4 years and the repo has been archived.
author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala The new version is missing at least half the subcommands the "old" completion script had and it also has no subcommand description.  And the "old" one is actually very up to date and was updated just last month: https://github.com/docker/cli/blob/7d723e2ba7b8607cf9ca5b3ea541f1ce60c700ec/contrib/completion/zsh/_docker#L6

At any rate, it makes no difference to me as I will not be using your version of the docker plugin and will be overriding it with the older version. But at a minimum, I recommend adding something to the README that explains why the new version is crippled until the new completion script generation feature with Cobra is implemented properly and how people can use the old version of the docker plugin to get better completion in the meantime and get it working if they have a version of docker older than 23.

Hi @rwmitchell! I understand your concern, but at this point removing emacs bindkeys it's a bit too breaking, it'd change many people environment.
It's not exactly true that it's the default: it is if it's a clean environment, but this is what docs says: `If one of the VISUAL or EDITOR environment variables contain the  string  `vi'  when  the shell starts up then it will be `viins', otherwise it will be `emacs'.  bindkey's -e and -v options provide a convenient way to override this default choice.`

author: dgvai

body:
Hi Chinar, thank you for the submission.

Unfortunately at this point I must reject the PR, here's my reasoning: currently there are many command-based AI tools that are making a case for their usefulness, and at this early stage it is not clear which will be more useful than the other, and therefore more suited for bundlind within OMZ.

We are striving to include tools that are going to make an impact for the vast majority of the users, and at this early stage we don't have a good sense of which ones are more relevant, or deserve our time and effort to maintain.

As more time happens, we might see that, indeed, this plugin is worth the effort and is something that our users demand to be included, and not something that they should install on their own.

For these reasons I'm going to close the PR, but we may revisit this decision in the future.

Thanks for the contribution!

I'm not quite sure that these aliases are quite relevant, actually. They are exactly the same as `good/bad`, and it's already kind of a standard to use `good/bad`, even though it makes complete sense what you say.
Taking it into account, it makes much more sense to me to have these kinds of aliases in your personal config, in case the semantics of `gbsg` and `gbsb` are not enough for you, or anyone in the same situation.
What do you kind about it?
author: carlosala

body:
I think that if `git bisect` were created from scratch, there would only be `old` and `new`. There’d be no need for `good` and `bad`, since they’re specific only to finding new bugs.

I’m hoping to encourage using `old` and `new` by default by teaching them first in my new book. I also thought this PR could help “spread the word”. Asking users to make custom aliases is a fine compromise, but I think it leaves the `good` and `bad` terms entrenched, despite their inferiority.
