body:
Hello James, thank you for the contribution.

I've reviewed the PR and I think at this stage we are not looking at getting at such a fine-grained level of customization. Doing so requires a level of hacky-ness that I don't really feel worth it or stylish, and is very coupled with the title logic.

We'll be looking in the future to revise this decision to see if we can improve this somehow. In the meantime, you can redefine the functions yourself in your zshrc file, or disable our solution by setting `DISABLE_AUTO_TITLE=true` and fully customize your logic while keeping compatibility with the `title` function.

Thanks again for your contribution
author: mcornella

body: Since autostashing can be always-enabled with [`merge.autoStash`](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config#Documentation/git-config.txt-mergeautoStash) and [`rebase.autoStash`](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config#Documentation/git-config.txt-rebaseautoStash), maybe an extra alias isn't worth it? Enable `rebase.autoStash` if `pull.rebase` is true, otherwise `merge.autoStash`, and all pulls will autostash. Using config also avoids duplicating all aliases with an autostash variant.

body: `--text` did however show patch differences for changes in _text_ files, which is missing now. This should perhaps use `-p` to show patch differences whenever there is a change in text files, without expanding binary differences, to more-or-less maintain ("backwards") compatibility with the original functionality provided by `gsts`. This also didn't appear to be considered/discussed in this PR.
author: MarijnS95

body:
Hi @MarijnS95!
According to `git` docs, `-p` is the default behaviour for `git show`, and indeed is appearing.
You can see the difference by executing `gsps HEAD` and `gsps -s HEAD`.
The only difference when removing `--text` is that binary files are not shown.
Thanks!
author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala `git show` != `git stash show` (and as such `gsps` != `gsts`).

From the `man git-stash` page under `show`:

> By default, the command shows **the diffstat**, but it will accept any format known to git diff (**e.g., `git stash show -p stash@{1}`** to view the second most recent entry **in patch form**). If no `<diff-option>` is provided, the default behavior will be given by the `stash.showStat`, and `stash.showPatch` config variables. You can also use `stash.showIncludeUntracked` to set whether `--include-untracked` is enabled by default.

(Emphasis mine)

Guess I will either override the `alias` or set `stash.showPatch` to get back the old behaviour.
author: MarijnS95

body:
Oh, I misread `gsps` instead of `gsts`.
I see the point, then! Even though I think `gsts` (in terms of semantics and stuff) is more configurable and gives the user the option to choose what they want each moment.
`gsts -p` might make sense in some situations!

body:
For now I have this in `.oh-my-zsh/custom/rtx.zsh`, along with the plugin activated:

```
if [[ -z "$RTX_DATA_DIR" ]]; then
	export RTX_DATA_DIR="${XDG_DATA_HOME:-${HOME}/.local/share}/rtx"
fi

path=("$RTX_DATA_DIR/shims" $path)
typeset -U path
```

body:
Hi Oscar, thank you for the contribution. However, we will not move forward with this suggestion, for the following reasons:

- We have already a ton of aliases for git, and we need to be more careful about which aliases we include.
- This alias can already be used via `gb | grep`, or using git branch completion in the command you'll be running (i.e. `git checkout <TAB>`).
- We don't see this alias to be run very frequently, so it is not worth the addition.
- As `| grep` is also very common, you can also define a global alias to more quickly add it to any commands, such as
```sh
alias -g :G='| grep'
# and then running something like
gb :G # expands to `git branch | grep`
```

If you still have a requirement for this alias, I'd suggest adding it to your own `.zshrc` file. Thanks!

LC_IDENTIFICATION="C"
LC_ALL=C
```
author: leoleoasd

body:
Hi Yuxuan, indeed you see that `LC_ALL` is setting the rest of values to `C` which does not support UTF-8, and therefore Zsh can't compute the symbols correctly unless explicitly forced. 

See [this FAQ entry](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/FAQ#how-do-i-change-my-locale) for instructions.

author: mcornella

body:
>The issue however, is that jq is not part of the standard tools on Linux or Mac, and I believe we could cater to a wider audience by not using jq.

Even though this is true:
- We do not care about MacOS: this path is only taken on wayland when `swaymsg` is present
- Even though `jq` is not installed by default, I'd argue that it's highly likely that if `sway` is being used, `jq` is installed, since it's both a convenient and very common tool used to process `swaymsg`'s json output.
- I'd rather maintain a `jq` oneliner rather than 20+ lines `awk`: I really don't think it's the right tool for the job.
author: bretello

body:
> * Even though `jq` is not installed by default, I'd argue that it's highly likely that if `sway` is being used, `jq` is installed, since it's both a convenient and very common tool used to process `swaymsg`'s json output.
> 

Doing a quick search in Archlinux's `sway` package, I don't see `jq` as a dependency (not even an optional dependency). I agree though that it may be a bit too much to have that `awk` script to just parse a JSON. On the other hand, it's not that long and would be really convenient for people that didn't installed `jq`.

Before merging, I'd like to set up support for tag updating.

Checking tags can be made with
```
# set up local repo with remote
# check remote tags
git ls-remote --tags
```
And checking out a specific tag with `--branch`, e.g.
```
git clone --depth=1 --branch v2.0 https://github.com/felipec/git-completion
```

author: zwimer

body:
Briefly, this function checks the following before deciding between `sudo -e` and `sudo $EDITOR`. For each argument:
1. Is the item a directory? If yes: `sudo $EDITOR`
1. Is the item a symlink? If yes: `sudo $EDITOR`
1. Does the parent directory exist? If no: `sudo $EDITOR`
1. Does the user own the parent directory? If yes: `sudo $EDITOR`
1. Does one of the user's groups own the parent directory? If yes: `sudo $EDITOR`

If no argument requires `sudo $EDITOR`, use `sudo -e`
author: zwimer

body:
Even more, this is the script that launches `ipython` in my system (`v8.2.0`).
```python
#!/usr/bin/python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re
import sys
from IPython import start_ipython
if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r"(-script\.pyw|\.exe)?$", "", sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(start_ipython())
```
We could simplify it to:
```zsh
alias ipython='python3 -c "import IPython, sys; sys.exit(IPython.start_ipython())"'
```

author: carlosala

body:
Ah, yes I think that's correct. As-is this change would break some functionality. 

I've asked [here](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/issues/3836#issuecomment-1959596650) to see if anyone might have a good idea.
author: detroyejr

body:
> Ah, yes I think that's correct. As-is this change would break some functionality.
> 
> I've asked [here](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/issues/3836#issuecomment-1959596650) to see if anyone might have a good idea.

I responded in that issue, that I think the alias needs to be turned into a function so it can leverage the `${VAR:+sub}` pattern.

For example, the `ta` alias will need to be converted to:

```zsh
function ta() {
    tmux attach-sessions ${1:+-t }$1
}
```

Other aliases using `-t` might need the same treatment.

The downside is of course that simply doing `alias` will no longer show `ta`.
author: pepoluan

body:
Thank you for the suggestion!

4 of the 7 aliases would need to be replaced with functions in order to work with 4.4. I've swapped those aliases out with something that I believe is the correct use of that pattern (let me know if you think otherwise). Looks like it works with both 3.3a and 3.4.

body:
Running `tmux new-session -s -d` creates a session named "-d". Others like `ta -d` attempts to attach to "-d" which doesn't feel exactly like a crash but I can add this in if we think this result is confusing.

Could you clarify what you think the result should be if a flag is passed?

If someone tries to run `ts -d`, should the response be some sort of message and early exit. For example,

```
function ts() {
  if [[ $1 == -* ]]; then
    print "Error: argument should not start with -" >&2
    return 1
  else
    tmux new-session ${1:+-s }$1
  fi
}
```

Or maybe just run `tmux <command> $1` and not pass the `-s` or `-t` flags?

author: matthew-nm

body:
For some reason, I still get the error I had previously. Just to be sure, does your `tmux -V` return 3.4?

Otherwise, I'm testing as follows:
```
# Reset
unfunction ta

function _build_tmux_alias {
  eval "if [[ -z \$1 ]] || [[ \${1:0:1} == '-' ]]; then
    alias \$1=\"tmux $2\"
  else
    alias \$1=\"tmux $2 $3\"
  fi"
}

_build_tmux_alias "ta" "attach" "-t"
```

Everything else works, but I still get `command attach-session: -t expects an argument` when trying to use `ta` without a session name.
author: detroyejr

body:
Yes, my `tmux -V` returns `tmux 3.4`.

And if I enter either of the commands `tmux attach-session` or `tmux attach-session -t` (no target specified for `-t`) it takes me to one of my running sessions. I'm on macOS Sonoma 14.2.1.

However, my Ubuntu workstation on tmux 3.2a doesn't like it when I don't have an argument for `-t`, but using your version of `ta` gets around that. I understand what your function is getting at now and it does seem to work properly for lower versions of tmux.

EDIT: And it's clear now that of course my snippet would not solve this problem, only your function addresses the issue for <3.4.
author: matthew-nm

body:
If you want more work ;) you could perhaps check for the tmux version and set the aliases to `tmux attach -t`, `tmux attach -d -t`, etc. if the version is >=3.4, and use the function approach otherwise. That way future autocomplete changes to tmux will come through directly via the aliases (which support empty targets), whereas lower versions get the benefit of empty target commands as well as the current implementation of autocompletes that you've duplicated.

Thanks for making this functional for a wide range of tmux's.

body: Thanks for confirming. That's weird because tmux didn't complain when I was on 3.2a (Linux/Nixos) and only started requiring an argument for `-t` after moving to 3.4. Maybe others can let us know which behavior they're seeing.

I have one question though in regard to the completion, where is this from? I haven't seen it anywhere else, and I can't see opentofu bundling it either. I'm hesitant to merge it this way over just doing

```sh
complete -C tofu tofu
```

which is the bash method and works for zsh as well\*, since the zsh way has to be kept up to date with the changes to `tofu`.

Is this just a rework of the completion in the terraform plugin? I've tested the `complete` method and it seems to do the same, minus the descriptions, so for maintainability I would rather go with the `complete` version.

\* After doing `bashcompinit`, which we do by default in Oh My Zsh.

author: brettmiller

body:
> Directly with no parameters.
> 
> ```
> $ history | wc -l
> 16
> $ which history
> history: aliased to omz_history -i
> ```

Since `history` is aliased to `omz_history -i` and not `omz_history`, you automatically send a parameter `-i`. To get the full history, you could run `history 1`. 

I think we should keep the parsing as is because there are many flags (`-d`, `-i`, `-f`, ...) that only change the format of the output of the command. However, another way of handling this would be to explicitly handle the flags that change environment variables (i.e. may have side-effects) differently and make all the other (format) flags print from 1. 

Please let me know what your thoughts are.
author: MoAlkhateeb

body:
I'm not setting the `history` alias to `omz_history -i` that's coming from lib/history.zsh. So, to me it makes since to explicitly  handle the aliases it sets as well. Testing locally adding 
```
  elif [[ $# -eq 1 ]]; then
    if [[ $1 -eq "-f" || $1 -eq "-E" || $1 -eq "-i" ]]; then
      builtin fc -l $1 1
    fi
```
between the `if [[ $# -eq 0 ]]; then` and `elif [[ -n "$clear" ]]; then` that was added in this PR seems to handle those cases correctly. I'd be happy to open a PR with that change.

author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala @septs Let me explain, I am a homebrew and oh-my-zsh user. Initially, my machine only had the BSD version of the nc command that comes with the Mac system.

```shell
which -a nc
/usr/bin/nc
```

shell-proxy is one of my favorite plugins, and I use it almost every day. One day, I installed a Homebrew version of nc.Because the Homebrew command in the PATH has a higher priority than the system default path, most of the time, it meets expectations to use the GNU version of commands.

```shell
which -a nc
/opt/homebrew/bin/nc
/usr/bin/nc
```

After some time, I discovered that shell-proxy was no longer working, but I did not realize that it was due to the previous Homebrew installation. with no choice, I became someone who manually exports the HTTP_PROXY setting. Until I could no longer tolerate it, I began to read the source code of shell-proxy, and after painstakingly debugging step by step, I discovered the aforementioned issue.

Setting the environment variable indeed solves my problem, but users similar to me might not infer from the error messages that the issue stems from having two versions of nc , specifically the differences between the BSD and GNU versions, causing the problem.

Of course, if you still believe that setting an environment variable is more appropriate, I can revise this PR (Pull Request) according to the previous suggestions. At the very least, the next time the plugin updates, I won't have to resolve conflicts locally.
author: vangie

body:
zmodload zsh/datetime

__timer_current_time() {
  echo $EPOCHREALTIME
}

__timer_format_duration() {
  local total_secs=$1
  local mins=$(printf '%.0f' $(echo "$total_secs / 60" | bc -l))
  local secs=$(printf "%.${TIMER_PRECISION:-1}f" $(echo "$total_secs - 60 * $mins" | bc -l))
  local duration_str="${mins}m${secs}s"
  local format="${TIMER_FORMAT:-/%d}"
  echo "${format//\%d/${duration_str#0m}}"
}

__timer_save_time_preexec() {
  __timer_cmd_start_time=$(__timer_current_time)
  __timer_last_command=$1
}

__timer_display_timer_precmd() {
  if [ -n "${__timer_cmd_start_time}" ] && [ "${__timer_last_command}" != "clear" ]; then
    local cmd_end_time=$(__timer_current_time)
    local tdiff=$(echo "$cmd_end_time - $__timer_cmd_start_time" | bc -l)
    unset __timer_cmd_start_time
    if [[ -z "${TIMER_THRESHOLD}" || $(echo "$tdiff >= $TIMER_THRESHOLD" | bc -l) -eq 1 ]]; then
      local tdiffstr=$(__timer_format_duration $tdiff)
      local cols=$((COLUMNS - ${#tdiffstr} - 1))
      printf "\033[1A\033[${cols}C %s\n" "$tdiffstr"
    fi
  fi
}

autoload -U add-zsh-hook
add-zsh-hook preexec __timer_save_time_preexec
add-zsh-hook precmd __timer_display_timer_precmd

author: mcornella

body:
> Does this fix an existing issue? If so, can you provide a link or reproduction steps?

If identities are set up via zstyle, identities are not checked. When loading the non-existing identity, `ssh-keygen -lf "$file"` will raise an exception: `ssh-keygen: /root/.ssh/$file: No such file or directory`.

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/ssh-agent/ssh-agent.plugin.zsh#L42-L50
By the way, if no identities are set up via zstyle, default identities are checked. Therefore, I think all identities should be checked.

body:
Hi @Ian729, thank you for the submission. Unfortunately this constitutes a breaking change, and the apple theme is pretty widely used. You can follow [this guide](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization#overriding-and-adding-themes) to copy the theme to the [`$ZSH_CUSTOM` folder](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Settings#zsh_custom) and changing its code.

A quick tip: what you need can be better achieved by using prompt sequences [[1]]:

```patch
diff --git a/themes/apple.zsh-theme b/themes/apple.zsh-theme
index 0c183258..fe292451 100644
--- a/themes/apple.zsh-theme
+++ b/themes/apple.zsh-theme
@@ -18,7 +18,7 @@ theme_precmd () {
 }
 
 setopt prompt_subst
-PROMPT='%{$fg[magenta]%}$(toon)%{$reset_color%} %~/ %{$reset_color%}${vcs_info_msg_0_}%{$reset_col>
+PROMPT='%(?.%{${fg[magenta]}%}.[%?]%{${fg[white]}%})$(toon)%{$reset_color%} %~/ %{$reset_color%}${>
 
 autoload -U add-zsh-hook
 add-zsh-hook precmd theme_precmd
```

Which would still give you the ability to override the `toon` function to put another symbol of your choosing.

[1]: https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Prompt-Expansion.html

body: Hey! Is there any changelog or something, or you know which is the first version of terminal-notifier that requires it? It'd be great to keep compatibility with older versions.
author: carlosala

body:
> Hey! Is there any changelog or something, or you know which is the first version of terminal-notifier that requires it? It'd be great to keep compatibility with older versions.

After `git blame` on the README, seems that [the docs about not using `-sender` and `-activate` together **dates back to 2013**](https://github.com/julienXX/terminal-notifier/commit/2d3e817fbb769ba20591d085ed64e025bed19a96). Which appears to be v1.5.2 . Latest is v2.0.0 but anyway more than ten years since this was documented as not correct.

Checked when did the usage of `-activate` and `-sender` started appearing all together in this plugin. [It dates back to 2015](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/7c31c7a96391e80bdd6ebdb6579751f4d44d6ac4).

Seems the issue [also exists](https://github.com/t413/zsh-background-notify/blob/d5f0430cb052f82c433c17707816910da87e201e/bgnotify.plugin.zsh#L39) in the [`t413/zsh-background-notify` repo](https://github.com/t413/zsh-background-notify) where this plugin comes from. With [an analogous PR offering same fix](https://github.com/t413/zsh-background-notify/pull/12)

My guess is it has been working until now despite not being the correct way of doing it. And maybe some macOS update dropped support for that. Just guessing though

body: I think that's a great addition. Partial clones make complete sense in the modern era of monorepos and, in general, incredibly huge repos. It makes more sense to me to just remove the `--also-filter-submodules` since we're already cloning them in a shallow way.
author: carlosala

body:
> I think that's a great addition. Partial clones make complete sense in the modern era of monorepos and, in general, incredibly huge repos. It makes more sense to me to just remove the `--also-filter-submodules` since we're already cloning them in a shallow way.

Thanks for the reply, @carlosala. I added the `--also-filter-submodules` to the alias when it was introduced with Git 2.36 while intentionally keeping `--shallow-submodules`. It serves the purpose of still getting the benefits of the filtered clone when `unshallowing` a submodule. For me personally, unshallowing submodules and working in them is a rare use case, but it has happened a few times. 

Maybe this is a missing piece of information that allows it to be left as is, otherwise please let me know if it should be reduced. Then I can push an update.

author: essembeh

body:
> Do you have some instructions to reproduce your error?

No worries, I just thought it might be helpful for you or others to mention it.

The issue occurred because I had `zsh_essembeh_gitstatus` in my custom prompt, and that function was removed in your recent update.

Erring prompt:
```
PROMPT='🌱 ~ ${ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX}%{$fg[$ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR]%}%n@%M%{$reset_color%}:%{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%} $(git_prompt_info)%(!.#.$) '
RPROMPT="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%?%{$reset_color%})"
PROMPT='🌱 %{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%} $(zsh_essembeh_gitstatus)%(!.#.$) '
RPROMPT="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%?%{$reset_color%})"
```

Fixed prompt:
```
PROMPT='🌱 ~ ${ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX}%{$fg[$ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR]%}%n@%M%{$reset_color%}:%{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%} $(git_prompt_info)%(!.#.$) '
RPROMPT="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%?%{$reset_color%})"
PROMPT='🌱 %{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%} $(git_prompt_info)%(!.#.$) '
RPROMPT="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%?%{$reset_color%})"
```
author: kjgenevieve

body:
> But `<<<` looks like strings added by git when you have conflicts when you pull from main... did you change this file locally?

You were right! This is the response from `cat essembeh.zsh-theme`:

```
# My custom theme:
#   - single line
#   - quite simple by default: user@host:$PWD
#   - green for local shell as non root
#   - red for ssh shell as non root
#   - magenta for root sessions
#   - prefix with remote address for ssh shells
#   - prefix to detect docker containers or chroot
#   - git plugin to display current branch and status

# git plugin
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX="%{$fg[cyan]%}("
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX=") %{$reset_color%}"


# by default, use green for user@host and no prefix
local ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR="green"
local ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX=""
if [[ -n "$SSH_CONNECTION" ]]; then
	# display the source address if connected via ssh
	ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX="%{$fg[yellow]%}[$(echo $SSH_CONNECTION | awk '{print $1}')]%{$reset_color%} "
	# use red color to highlight a remote connection
	ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR="red"
elif [[ -r /etc/debian_chroot ]]; then
	# prefix prompt in case of chroot
	ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX="%{$fg[yellow]%}[chroot:$(cat /etc/debian_chroot)]%{$reset_color%} "
elif [[ -r /.dockerenv ]]; then
	# also prefix prompt inside a docker container
	ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX="%{$fg[yellow]%}[docker]%{$reset_color%} "
fi
if [[ $UID = 0 ]]; then
	# always use magenta for root sessions, even in ssh
	ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR="magenta"
fi
<<<<<<< Updated upstream
PROMPT='${ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX}%{$fg[$ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR]%}%n@%M%{$reset_color%}:%{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%} $(git_prompt_info)%(!.#.$) '
=======
# PROMPT=':seedling: ~ '
PROMPT='🌱 ~
${ZSH_ESSEMBEH_PREFIX}%{$fg[$ZSH_ESSEMBEH_COLOR]%}%n@%M%{$reset_color%}:%{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%}
$(zsh_essembeh_gitstatus)%(!.#.$) '
RPROMPT="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%?%{$reset_color%})"
PROMPT='🌱 %{%B$fg[yellow]%}%~%{$reset_color%b%}
$(zsh_essembeh_gitstatus)%(!.#.$) '
>>>>>>> Stashed changes
RPROMPT="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%?%{$reset_color%})"
```

I didn't touch the file, but I wonder if running `source ~/.zshrc` could have done something? At any rate, it's working now! Thank you so much for your help.

author: alexrosenfeld10

body:
Hi Clay, appreciate your contribution and for letting us know about this, but unfortunately this does not make sense.

The global alias suggested is bad for interoperability, and you will find other issues having it set (for example, in some commands `-h` means "human" display, or sort).

It's better to fix the global alias, for example changing it to the `:h` or `:help` or any other non-common symbol. `-h` condition is the POSIX one.

Thanks for the contribution though.
author: mcornella

body:
Oh, I'm aware of the possibilities of conflicts and accept there may be unwanted side-effects. I've encountered very few actual issues, just a couple small things like this.

This PR was just switching from one conditional (`-h`) to another (`-L`) with the exact same effect to work around a minor side-effect that only occurs when loading ohmyzsh a second time in the same session. I'm happy to just keep my own fork -- it's a tiny diff -- but thought it could help future users from seeing/reporting an issue with ohmyzsh that could be easily avoided.

Thanks for your work maintaining ohmyzsh, by the way! It's very useful.

body:
@mikhailde this pull request prevents the loading of my RSA key (saved as PEM), which is still valid.

This begins with:

```
-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
```

and ends with:

```
-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
```

It can be converted with `ssh-keygen -p -f ~/.ssh/id_rsa`.
author: mnin

body: @mnin Thanks for reporting this issue! I've addressed the problem with loading RSA keys in PEM format in a new pull request: #12758. This PR uses a more robust method for identifying private keys that should correctly handle your RSA key. Please check it out and let me know if it resolves the issue for you.
author: mikhailde

body: @mikhailde thank you! Was on the way to do that too :-)
author: mnin

body: Should this be the default behavior? Users who have many PEM files in the .ssh directory are now flooded with key loads during terminal startup.
author: michelmzs

body:
Thanks for raising this concern.

The recent change aims to improve flexibility by supporting a wider range of key names and PEM files in the `.ssh` directory. I understand that this might lead to more key loading messages for some users.

However, you can easily customize the plugin's behavior using `zstyle`. You can add these `zstyle` commands to your `.zshrc` file to make them persistent. For instance, if you only want to load specific keys, you can specify them explicitly:

```zsh
zstyle :omz:plugins:ssh-agent identities id_rsa id_ed25519 my_custom_key
```

Alternatively, you can suppress the loading messages during startup with:

```zsh
zstyle :omz:plugins:ssh-agent quiet yes
```

These options and other customization possibilities are detailed in the plugin's wiki page. You can check them out and tailor the plugin to your specific needs.
author: mikhailde

body:
Hi! We decided to rollback to the previous settings. If users want to load other keys than the default they can add them to `identities` option. There were many downsides of this change raised in many issues and we decided that the previous behaviour was good enough.
Thanks for everyone's contributions!
author: carlosala

body: Thanks for the update, Carlos. I understand the decision to revert the change. It's unfortunate that it caused some issues, but I appreciate the effort to maintain a stable and reliable experience for all users. I'll keep the code in case it can be useful in the future.

The thing is this will show the information for **all** cluster pods, including system ones. 

There are no other aliases that do this, so this definitely does not bring the same value as the other ones. 

This question is also designed to know whether this is a regular use case of other kubectl users, mainly because we have tons of other alias requests which often conflict with other aliases or commands. So when you tick the box that you've provided such justification, we expect it to be there.

This alias is not much shorter than just doing `kdp -A`. Thanks for the taking the time to make a contribution, but the justification is not enough for one more alias, so I'm going to reject this one.

body:
There are two purposes: (1) efficiency, being considerably faster to display dirty-or-clean, depending on repo size and modifications, with "hg identify" than storing the full "hg status" for grep, and (2) showing the current branch, bookmark, and tags -- blanking out each if not used, and customizable with the IDENTIFY variable.

> Does this change the visual behaviour of the mercurial prompt?

In addition to the second above, and after reviewing it fresh now: yes, a tiny visual change (unbolded magenta and bolded yellow).  That is customizable with the same variables as previously used.

body:
Note: how to generate the zsh autocompletion file:

```
echo "autoload -U compinit; compinit" >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc
stratus completion zsh > "${fpath[1]}/_stratus"
```

Ref: https://stratus-red-team.cloud/user-guide/commands/autocompletion/#manually

body:
There is actual OSC52 support for [pasting](https://github.com/milhnl/dotfiles/blob/9d694cc936345217b3fbcb4d91332ddfded62ddd/XDG_BIN_HOME/vis-clipboard#L22). But I'm not sure if we want to implement that because many terminal emulators have disabled it as default for security reasons.

Also, I would rather do nothing than print a text if pasting is triggered.

In my personal config I ended up with `printf "\033]52;c;%s\a" "$(head -c 838860 "${1:-/dev/stdin}" | base64 -w 0)"` which also ensures that the copied content isn't larger than 8MB which seems to be a limit for OSC52 (see [here](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/changelog/)). 

If I ssh to a system is there always   `"$TERM" == "xterm"* || "$TERM" == "screen"* || "$TERM" == "tmux"*` set? Maybe we could get rid of the existing `else` and just use OSC52 if none of the other cases matched....

author: carlosala

body:
I use direnv with p10k. P10k specifically [calls out how you should use direnv with insta-prompt](https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k?tab=readme-ov-file#how-do-i-initialize-direnv-when-using-instant-prompt):

```
(( ${+commands[direnv]} )) && emulate zsh -c "$(direnv export zsh)"

if [[ -r "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh" ]]; then
  source "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh"
fi

(( ${+commands[direnv]} )) && emulate zsh -c "$(direnv hook zsh)"
```

Another thing you can do if you don't want the conflict is just change `POWERLEVEL9K_INSTANT_PROMPT` from verbose to quiet in your `~/.p10k.zsh` file.

```
  # Instant prompt mode.
  #
  #   - off:     Disable instant prompt. Choose this if you've tried instant prompt and found
  #              it incompatible with your zsh configuration files.
  #   - quiet:   Enable instant prompt and don't print warnings when detecting console output
  #              during zsh initialization. Choose this if you've read and understood
  #              https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k#instant-prompt.
  #   - verbose: Enable instant prompt and print a warning when detecting console output during
  #              zsh initialization. Choose this if you've never tried instant prompt, haven't
  #              seen the warning, or if you are unsure what this all means.
  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_INSTANT_PROMPT=quiet
```

Perhaps a more useful plugin might be one like this:

```
# p10k-instant-prompt.plugin.zsh
[[ "$ZSH_THEME" == powerlevel10k/powerlevel10k ]] || return 1

if zstyle -t ':omz:plugins:p10k-instant-prompt' use-direnv; then
  (( ${+commands[direnv]} )) && emulate zsh -c "$(direnv export zsh)"
fi

if [[ -r "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh" ]]; then
  source "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh"
fi

if zstyle -t ':omz:plugins:p10k-instant-prompt' use-direnv; then
  (( ${+commands[direnv]} )) && emulate zsh -c "$(direnv hook zsh)"
fi
```

body:
I have also executed `asdf reshim`. But I still only get the message `zsh: command not found: node` when I type `node` into my terminal.

Now I have added a few debug messages to the original plugin:
```shell
# Find where asdf should be installed
ASDF_DIR="${ASDF_DIR:-$HOME/.asdf}"
ASDF_COMPLETIONS="$ASDF_DIR/completions"


__debug()
{
    local file="$OMZ_ASDF_DEBUG_FILE"
    if [[ -n ${file} ]]; then
        echo "$*" >> "${file}"
    fi
}

__debug "\n========= starting asdf ohmyzsh plugin =========="
__debug "\nASDF_DIR: ${ASDF_DIR}"
__debug "\nASDF_COMPLETIONS: ${ASDF_COMPLETIONS}"

if [[ ! -f "$ASDF_DIR/asdf.sh" || ! -f "$ASDF_COMPLETIONS/_asdf" ]]; then
  __debug "\n$ASDF_DIR/asdf.sh or $ASDF_COMPLETIONS/_asdf not found"
  __debug "\ncheck for archlinux/AUR package (/opt/asdf-vm/) and Homebrew package‚"
  # If not found, check for archlinux/AUR package (/opt/asdf-vm/)
  if [[ -f "/opt/asdf-vm/asdf.sh" ]]; then
    __debug "/opt/asdf-vm/asdf.sh found"
    ASDF_DIR="/opt/asdf-vm"
    ASDF_COMPLETIONS="$ASDF_DIR"
  # If not found, check for Homebrew package
  elif (( $+commands[brew] )); then
    __debug "\nhomebrew package found"
    _ASDF_PREFIX="$(brew --prefix asdf)"
    ASDF_DIR="${_ASDF_PREFIX}/libexec"
    ASDF_COMPLETIONS="${_ASDF_PREFIX}/share/zsh/site-functions"
    __debug "\n_ASDF_PREFIX: ${_ASDF_PREFIX}"
    __debug "\n_ASDF_DIR: ${ASDF_DIR}"
    __debug "\n_ASDF_COMPLETIONS: ${ASDF_COMPLETIONS}"
    __debug "\nASDF diretory:\n$(tree -a --dirsfirst ${_ASDF_PREFIX})"
    unset _ASDF_PREFIX
  else
    __debug "\narchlinux/AUR package nor Homebrew package not found"
    return
  fi
fi

# Load command
__debug "\nTry to load command $ASDF_DIR/asdf.sh"
if [[ -f "$ASDF_DIR/asdf.sh" ]]; then
  __debug "\n Load command $ASDF_DIR/asdf.sh"
  source "$ASDF_DIR/asdf.sh"
  # Load completions
  if [[ -f "$ASDF_COMPLETIONS/_asdf" ]]; then
    __debug "\n Add completions to fpath ($ASDF_COMPLETIONS)"
    fpath+=("$ASDF_COMPLETIONS")
    autoload -Uz _asdf
    compdef _asdf asdf # compdef is already loaded before loading plugins
  fi
fi

__debug "\n========= finished asdf ohmyzsh plugin =========="
```

Here is the output:
```log
========= starting asdf ohmyzsh plugin ==========

ASDF_DIR: /Users/valgard/.asdf

ASDF_COMPLETIONS: /Users/valgard/.asdf/completions

/Users/valgard/.asdf/asdf.sh or /Users/valgard/.asdf/completions/_asdf not found

check for archlinux/AUR package (/opt/asdf-vm/) and Homebrew package‚

homebrew package found

_ASDF_PREFIX: /opt/homebrew/opt/asdf

_ASDF_DIR: /opt/homebrew/opt/asdf/libexec

_ASDF_COMPLETIONS: /opt/homebrew/opt/asdf/share/zsh/site-functions

ASDF diretory:
/opt/homebrew/opt/asdf
├── .brew
│   └── asdf.rb
├── bin
│   └── asdf
├── etc
│   └── bash_completion.d
│       └── asdf
├── share
│   ├── fish
│   │   └── vendor_completions.d
│   │       └── asdf.fish
│   └── zsh
│       └── site-functions
│           └── _asdf
├── CHANGELOG.md
├── INSTALL_RECEIPT.json
├── LICENSE
├── README.md
└── sbom.spdx.json

10 directories, 10 files

Try to load command /opt/homebrew/opt/asdf/libexec/asdf.sh

========= finished asdf ohmyzsh plugin ==========
```

And as you can see, the `/opt/homebrew/opt/asdf/libexec/asdf.sh` does not exist and therefore cannot be loaded.

According to the official documentation https://asdf-vm.com/guide/upgrading-to-v0-16.html you have to include the `shim` directory in the path. In addition, the binary of asdf v016.x is located under `bin` and no longer under `libexec`.

I use Homebrew and asdf on an Apple Silicon with macOS 15.x.

author: mmurdockk

body: This patch is appending `$ASDF_DATA_DIR/shims` to the end of `path`. So the system binaries in `/usr/bin` are all taking precedence.
author: jhclouse

body:
> This patch is appending `$ASDF_DATA_DIR/shims` to the end of `path`. So the system binaries in `/usr/bin` are all taking precedence.

I have now changed the code so that the shims path is prepended.
author: Valgard

body: Thanks everyone, this has been fixed! I took a different approach to it, leaving the code as-is if new asdf is found. Thanks!

author: fabricionaweb

body:
This is a really good idea!

What do you think about renaming the command to `view` and having a `-w` argument opening the plugin folder in GitHub, as other CLIs do? 

Also, we have to take into consideration plugins with multiple files, I'm not sure what we'd do then.
author: mcornella

body:
I can definitely switch to `view` from `cat` if that's what you would prefer.

RE the `-w` argument; do you have a recommendation on how to programmatically/consistently refer to the plugin's folder on Github?

Lastly, can you send an example of a plugin that has multiple files?

Thank you!
author: emiliadecaudin

body:
> I can definitely switch to `view` from `cat` if that's what you would prefer.

I think that's a good established pattern now, for example in [`gh`](https://cli.github.com/manual/gh_repo_view) or [`glab`](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/cli/-/blob/main/docs/source/repo/view.md).

> RE the `-w` argument; do you have a recommendation on how to programmatically/consistently refer to the plugin's folder on Github?

The URL will be `https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/tree/master/` + path to plugin (`plugins/<plugin-name>`). The first part will be hardcoded probably, and we might need to change the `<master>` branch in the future if we ever apply #9015, though GitHub will redirect anyway.

> Lastly, can you send an example of a plugin that has multiple files?

We have a bunch that don't have a `.plugin.zsh`, usually only containing completion files (`_<plugin-name>`).

author: joyfulai

body:
tl;dr: We don't have any workaround for zero additional package?

---

I'm macOS user and I found that my macbook uses en1 instead of en0, as you said. Your patch makes sense to some extent.

But is it the only solution for us to require another package? As a normal omz user, I'm a bit worried about this solution might demand excessive to some users, including me. It would be a little frustrated if I enable **`systemadmin`** then computer said **"ip:2: command not found: ip"**.
author: roeniss

body:
> tl;dr: We don't have any workaround for zero additional package?

It's an improvement over the previous situation which didn't work at all on macOS. I can either work on an additional PR that uses `route get` on macOS, or if you prefer I'll revise this one.

I thought it best to get the simplest portable solution out first so as not to get bogged down over the mechanism to detect what platform we’re running on.

body:
> Sorry for interrupt, can you tell me some examples that will be changed with this PR?

Now instead of executing `man`, it executes `command man`, which runs the default `man` command without taking aliases into account. So previously, if you made `man` an alias to `man-preview` it would try to run itself recursively and just get stuck like that. So basically this PR just fixes that.

body:
I have no idea what name would be the best.... flip-flop? fancy-ctrl-z? back-and-forth-fg? Haha. anyway the idea is so cool. 

I always use 2-split pane strategy (w/ iTerm2) for this situation, because I don't want my two job to be suspended (stopped) (e.g., one pane for curl request, one pane for docker logs -f). Your use case, on the other hand, have no problem with suspended jobs, right?
author: roeniss

body:
> I have no idea what name would be the best.... flip-flop? fancy-ctrl-z? back-and-forth-fg? Haha. anyway the idea is so cool.

`fancy-ctrl-z` is the name of a plugin already in the repository. I do not think adding a whole new plug-in for this would be a better strategy. 🙂 

> I always use 2-split pane strategy (w/ iTerm2) for this situation, because I don't want my two job to be suspended (stopped) (e.g., one pane for curl request, one pane for docker logs -f).

I could not use split panes for the following reasons.

1. The terminal I'm using does not directly implement pane-based viewports. (Tabs are a recent invention there, but it opened up a can of worms for which I had to roll back to an older version.) I could use `tmux` but because I have very fancy things in my Vim configuration, `tmux`'s inability to understand a few custom VT sequences (that my terminal **does** understand) would lessen the experience.
2. When I do use parallel workflows I open two terminals and use one for this, one for that. Usually, I do use that, one terminal runs the editor(s), the other runs the build / execution. So I don't background my editor to run a build or observe logs.

Sometimes the "main" screen (I call it main as that is the one directly in front of me) has one editor and one `htop` (usually the htop is backgrounded), because sometimes I need to kill the build. But often it's 2 editors for two separate projects I have to wrangle at the same time. If I have to read or change both projects, it's always a sequence of "this uses that, let's go over to see how that one does it".

> Your use case, on the other hand, have no problem with suspended jobs, right?

No. I've found Vim to be very good at handling SIGTSTP, and generally for plugins in Vim which run subprocesses (LSP services, debugger, etc.) are usually cascade-suspended there. Also, for `fancy-ctrl-z` to activate and work, it must have been the user's prior conscious action to have suspended a process.
author: whisperity

body:
I understood all your saying. Thank you for the sincere answer. I still have one question.

I think the "exactly two" condition is quite cruel. How about "cyclic switching"?

- If there's one background jobs, I can visit it with 2 `ctrl-z` (fg 1, bg)
- If there's two background jobs, I can visit two background jobs in turn with 4 `ctrl-z` (fg 1, bg, fg 2, bg)
- If there's three background jobs, I can visit all three background jobs in turn with 6 `ctrl-z` (fg 1, bg, fg 2, bg, fg 3, bg)
- ...

I’m not the maintainer, so consider this a casual suggestion please :D

P.S., At first, I thought that `ctrl-z` will switch between two jobs without "bg" i.e., fg 1 -> fg 2 -> fg 1 -> ...
author: roeniss

body:
> How about "cyclic switching"?
> 
>    * If there's one background jobs, I can visit it with 2 `ctrl-z` (fg 1, bg)
> 
>    * If there's two background jobs, I can visit two background jobs in turn with 4 `ctrl-z` (fg 1, bg, fg 2, bg)
> 
>    * If there's three background jobs, I can visit all three background jobs in turn with 6 `ctrl-z` (fg 1, bg, fg 2, bg, fg 3, bg)
> 
>    * ...

I don't think we can do this without a lot of additional work. First, `fancy-ctrl-z` would need a way to keep information which was the last visited job (in order to know at which point int the cycle we are at). But jobs can be terminated in the middle of the cycle (have 3 jobs, `fg %2`, then exit). If you terminate a job in the middle of a cycle, the shell with reuse the ID. So if you suspend 3 jobs and then exit the 2nd and create a new – essentially 4th – job, it will get ID 2, so the cycle is "broken" in a sense. But the shell doesn't give you any additional shortcuts apart from `%N` where $N$ is the ID, or `%+` / `%-` where the `%+` refers to the most recently suspended job, and `%-` refers to the second to most recently suspended one. We would have to reimplement a lot of and around the job control mechanism purely in a shell script, which would also not work well if the job control is interfaced with outside of the plug-in.

- fancy-ctrl-z: open b

- fancy-ctrl-z: open c

author: roeniss

body:
@roeniss Thank you for the comments! Admittedly, this fell off my radar for quite some time. I only realised this PR is pending when I made my newer PR #13513 and found that on my machine the OMZ directory that was actually being used by the shell still had this PR checked out as opposed to the `master` branch 😬.

I've given it some thought and also found some bugs related to the current implementation. For example, if you did

```zsh
$ vim a.txt
<background>
$ vim b.txt
<background>
$ cd /tmp
$ ^Z
```

it only ever brought forth `vim b.txt`, which is **clearly a bug** and I'm on track of fixing it.

> Should we have "exactly 2" condition still? Why not just use "last 2"?
> 
> why this can't be activated with 3 background jobs.

Actually, I've given it some thought and tested a few things. With my previous mentions of `%+` and `%-`, it's clear that the Shell can give information about which was the "last 2" **suspended** jobs.
We can think about this like on TV remotes there used to be a button which switched you from the current channel to the channel you viewed prior. So if you went to channel 2 and then 8, pressing the button got you 2, 8, 2, 8, … again. But if after 2, 8, you went to channel 17, then 8, 17, 8, …; it forgot about the "2".

Several tools and utilities support using the `-` marker to explicitly intent switching to the "most recent" thing. For example, `cd -` will switch between your current and your most recent directory, while `git checkout -` will switch between your current and your most recently checked out branch.

And, in fact unlike TV remotes (😂), the shell keeps a stack of this information much better, as if we have 3 suspended jobs and one gets foregrounded and exits, it will return the `%+`, `%-` markers to how they have been previously. Importantly, as well, running background jobs do not "meddle" with this state.

>  * a: added to bg

I think we should be a bit clearer here for the record. There is a difference between "job in foreground/background" vs. "job is running/suspended". Likely 99% of the "foreground" jobs have to be "running", and I can't think of a reasonable case where a foreground job is suspended. However, there can be "background + running" jobs! Everything you start with a `&` or explicitly `bg %N` after a suspension is **running**, just in the background. The simplest explanation for "background" is that their standard stream operations (mounted to the pseudo-tty) would result in _SIGTTIN_ or _SIGTTOU_, which some processes handle well, others do not.

In fact, background-running processes also mislead and caused bugs in the implementation as it is in this patch now.

For example, `yes` can perfectly operate in the background (and it keeps spamming your terminal with `y` lines), but `cat` (no arguments, read from _stdin_, print to _stdout_!) immediately suspends itself when it is attempted to be backgrounded.

Here's a little test:

```zsh
~$ vim A.txt
<suspend>
~$ vim B.txt
<suspend>
~$ jobs
[1]  - suspended vim A.txt
[2]  + suspended vim B.txt
# Clearly, B is the last, and A is the second-to-last job here.
~$ yes >/dev/null &
~$ jobs
[1]  - suspended vim A.txt
[2]  + suspended vim B.txt
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
~$ cd Desktop/
Desktop$ jobs
[1]  - suspended vim A.txt
[2]  + suspended vim B.txt
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
Desktop$ jobs -d
[1]  - suspended vim A.txt
(pwd : ~)
[2]  + suspended vim B.txt
(pwd : ~)
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
(pwd : ~)
Desktop$ cat &
Desktop$ jobs
[1]    suspended             vim A.txt
[2]  - suspended             vim B.txt
[3]    running               yes >/dev/null
[4]  + suspended (tty input) cat
# Now 'cat' is the last, and 'B' is the second-to-last job.
Desktop$ cd ~/
~$ jobs
[1]    suspended             vim A.txt
[2]  - suspended             vim B.txt
[3]    running               yes >/dev/null
[4]  + suspended (tty input) cat
~$ fg
# Equivalent to "fg %+", taking the last suspended job back to the foreground.
[4]    continued             cat
(pwd : ~/Desktop)
cat: stdin: interrupted system call
(pwd now: ~)
# 'cat' exited with 1.
~$ jobs
[1]  - suspended vim A.txt
[2]  + suspended vim B.txt
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
```

So, yes, we can do "switch between last two suspended jobs" just fine, with what information the Shell can currently provide to us. Foregrounding a non-suspended background job is not that easy (for reasons mentioned, it would require a lot of additional scripting, maybe even additional features added to Zsh itself!) unambiguously. Having multiple jobs with an intermediate one exiting will leave a "slot" open which gets reused by a later-spawned job, so the `%N` ID of the jobs (neither their system PIDs) are good indicator of order:

```zsh
~$ jobs
[1]  - suspended vim A.txt
[2]  + suspended vim B.txt
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
~$ vim C.txt
<suspend>
~$ jobs
[1]    suspended vim A.txt
[2]  - suspended vim B.txt
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
[4]  + suspended vim C.txt
~$ fg %3
[3]    running   yes >/dev/null
^C
# yes exits with SIGINT (-2).
~$ jobs
[1]    suspended vim A.txt
[2]  - suspended vim B.txt
[4]  + suspended vim C.txt
~$ vim D.txt
<suspend>
~$ jobs
[1]    suspended vim A.txt
[2]    suspended vim B.txt
[3]  + suspended vim D.txt
[4]  - suspended vim C.txt
```

This patch will change _`fancy-ctrl-z`_ 's behaviour slightly, as the original intent was only to switch back to _the_ (?) background (slightly problematic terminology here again) job. But I think the use of _"fancy"_ is elevated greatly by this feature.

body:
Hmm. At the time, I was under the impression that those 3 aliases were to be replaced by their corresponding functions that have also been there for quite a long time now. Was following up on this commit https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/207d29b716301c77392ae140b1482eeba44e9102 that started deprecation notices, which seemed like the perfect opportunity to proceed with it. `ggpur` was already just an alias for the function `ggu`. The aliases are still there, I just had them prefixed with the deprecation text, is behavior still inconsistent outside of the extra message, like not passing flags? Completion should work just fine too.

Honestly, I didn't think this PR would get merged. Been bumping this regularly for months to no reply, but I've been fine staying on the branch locally. If users really feel strongly about this, then that's my bad for misinterpreting the maintainers' intentions and user concensus. Ultimately though, these discussions can only happen when the changes are merged, so comments are always appreciated.

author: adamaps

body:
> @AYO-YO / @carlosala
> 
> I really like the look of this in agnoster, but I'm now seeing duplicated conda environment labels (legacy label shown in brackets).
> 
> ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/92136848/454476347-b97bb847-2f5b-401b-9dbc-6ec30f722a22.png?jwt=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..DzqbWadb1JUDWHoFuR5nmlwtMlkmnLKim0wMtFt530w)
> 
> What information do you need to help me resolve this?

You can execute the command `conda config --set changeps1 False` to disable conda's own prompt display. After executing the command, reload your user profile to make it take effect.

Example:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/48e9d239-5451-42c8-a0e3-c90e351c2b4e)

author: ohmyzsh

body:
The `genpass-*` generators are all specifically targeting 128 bits of symmetric security, this why `genpass-apple` is not 20 characters. Rather, the standard 20 characters only provides about 72 bits: log2(17 * 19^11 * 6^6 * 10 * 6) ≈ 72.231.

However, it does adhere to [Apple's password rules validation tool](https://developer.apple.com/password-rules/), just enforcing the pronounceable `cvvcvc` structure..
author: atoponce

body:
> The `genpass-*` generators are all specifically targeting 128 bits of symmetric security, this why `genpass-apple` is not 20 characters. Rather, the standard 20 characters only provides about 72 bits: log2(17 * 19^11 * 6^6 * 10 * 6) ≈ 72.231.
> 
> However, it does adhere to [Apple's password rules validation tool](https://developer.apple.com/password-rules/), just enforcing the pronounceable `cvvcvc` structure..

The spec is described [here](https://rmondello.com/2024/10/07/apple-passwords-generated-strong-password-format/) by the developer at Apple who designed it. They target 71 bits. Why shouldn't this plugin behave like the apple password generator?
author: ryanvo

body: Because I designed it to provide security for cryptographic symmetric keys which is at least 128 bits.
author: atoponce

body: What if we made the number of words a parameter? My use case is to generate passwords exactly like the Apple Passwords app in a non-Mac environment. I understand your requirement, but the Apple spec is a practical balance.
author: ryanvo

body:
Outside of the argument to generate multiple passwords, I'd rather not implement any additional command line parsing. The beauty of the way it's designed now is it is both convenient _and_ secure. There are no foot guns in the plugin that weaken or compromise security. If you want a weaker password, you have to make the conscious decision to do that outside of the tool.

The password is hyphenated, so it should be trivial to pipe it to something like `sed(1)` or `awk(1)` in your scripts to trim it down if you want a shorter version that strictly adheres with the 72-bit/20-character implementation generated by iOS and Safari.
author: atoponce

body:
> The password is hyphenated, so it should be trivial to pipe it to something like `sed(1)` or `awk(1)` in your scripts to trim it down if you want a shorter version that strictly adheres with the 72-bit/20-character implementation generated by iOS and Safari.

I'd rather say this is a good point, let's stick to the current implementation.
Thanks for your contribution!

body: After using jj a bit more recently, one obvious alias to add to this list is `jjnt="jj new 'trunk()'"`. I often work with repos that have either master or main as their head branch name and forget which exact one to use. Having a single command that does the right thing would be useful. This alias is useful when finishing work on one feature and starting on the next. Typing `trunk()` requires the quotes as the name is interpreted as a function name by default in zsh / bash.

body:
Hi, thanks for your work !

I'm not a big contributor to oh-my-zsh, but I did some work and review for the `git` plugin.

I would like to ship some `gh` aliases, too! However, I think we can refine the approach here.

First, you've aliased most of the `gh` subcommands, but I think we should limit the list to the most popular 10 or so aliases, especially at first. The `gh auth login` command, for example, is pretty useless, as it only needs to be run once, when setting up `gh`. I also think most of the shell functions could be removed.

Second, some key aliases are missing, at least `gh issue develop --checkout`. That's a very neat workflow command for going from issue number to local branch ready to make a PR.

Third, I think placing the aliases in the existing `gh` plugin would be fine.
author: adamchainz

body:
Yes, thanks! I mostly agree.
It's very personal. I actually have many usecases for ghat, just tody I did this:
```
docker run -i --rm \
  -e GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN=$(ghat) \
  -e GITHUB_DYNAMIC_TOOLSETS=1 \
  ghcr.io/github/github-mcp-server
```

Looking at the full plugin, I see an opportunity for customization. 
Do you know of any plugins that allows to opt-in/out of features?

Given that the Oh My Zsh alias almost certainly predates that tool, the priority should probably be on stability for existing users.

We don't have usage metrics, so I'm not sure to revert or not. I could either revert, keep the change or add logic to only rename if kl is installed.

It's true that kl seems less popular and less standardized than other commands for which we typically make these changes, but I'm also not sure if the alias was ingrained in users' workflows.

I'll wait for more feedback to make a decision. Feel free to share this PR with any people you know that might be affected by the change. Thanks

I get, that the soft rule is to avoid conflicts, however I see two issues with how things currently are. First, if `kl` was renamed, then `klf` should be renamed too. How about this?

`path=("$ASDF_DATA_DIR/shims" ${path:#$ASDF_DATA_DIR/shims})`

I updated the assignment so that it will prepend the shims entry to the path array with any entries matching the shims path removed. Your suggestion was great, and I think this is even cleaner than what I had before. This ensures that there aren't duplicate entries in the path and that it's the first entry in the array.

--fork-point                                    -- use merge-base --fork-point to refine upstream
--gpg-sign                                      -- GPG-sign the commit
--ignore-whitespace                             -- ignore changes in whitespace
--interactive                                   -- make a list of commits to be rebased and open in $EDITOR
--keep-base                                     -- use the merge-base of upstream and branch as the current base
--merge                                         -- use merging strategies to rebase
--no-autostash                                  -- don't stash uncommitted changes before rebasing and apply them afterwar
--no-ff                                         -- cherry-pick all rebased commits with --interactive, otherwise synonymou
--no-gpg-sign                                   -- don't GPG-sign the commit
--no-stat                                       -- don't show diffstat of what changed upstream
--no-verify                                     -- bypass the pre-rebase hook
--onto                                          -- start new branch with HEAD equal to given revision
--quiet                                         -- suppress all output
--reapply-cherry-picks                          -- apply all changes, even those already present upstream
--rebase-merges                                 -- try to rebase merges instead of skipping them
--rerere-autoupdate                             -- update the index with reused conflict resolution if possible
--reschedule-failed-exec                        -- automatically re-schedule any 'exec' that fails
--reset-author-date              --ignore-date  -- ignore author date and use current date
--root                                          -- rebase all reachable commits
--signoff                                       -- add Signed-off-by: trailer to the commit message
--strategy                                      -- use given merge strategy
--strategy-option                               -- pass merge-strategy-specific option to merge strategy
--verbose                                       -- output additional information
--whitespace                                    -- detect a new or modified line that has whitespace errors
```

author: mcornella

body:
Hi Marc, thanks for the review!

The aliases in this plugin are designed to make working with `glab` faster and more convenient. Here’s how I would personally use them in daily workflows:

- **`gl`** - shorthand for `glab`. Use to save keystrokes when running any `glab` command.
- **`glmr`** - shorthand for `glab mr`. Run `glmr-list` to see merge requests assigned and `glmr-checkout <MR>` to start working on a branch.
- **`glissue`** - shorthand for `glab issue`. Use `glissue-new "Bug in login"` to quickly create issues and `glissue-list` to track open issues.
- **`glrepo`** - shorthand for `glab repo`. Use `glrepo-clone <project>` for cloning repositories and `glrepo-open <project>` to open a repo in the browser quickly.
- **`glci`** - shorthand for `glab ci`. Can use `glci-latest` to check the most recent pipeline and `glci-retry <pipeline>` if a job fails.
- **`glprj`** - shorthand for `glab project`. Use it to view project details without typing the full command.
- **`glrelease`** - shorthand for `glab release`. Use `glrelease-create "v1.2.3" "v1.2.3"` to quickly tag and release new versions.

Overall, these aliases save time from repeatedly typing `glab` subcommands and make helper functions easier to call in common workflows.

Additionally, I’ve updated some of the helper functions to work with the latest version of glab. For example:

- Removed unsupported flags like `--state opened` from `glissue-list`.
- Updated `glrelease-create` to use `--name instead` of `-t` for specifying the release title.
- Verified other functions (`glmr-merge`, `glci-latest`, etc.) to ensure compatibility with the current `glab` CLI.
- These changes ensure the plugin works with newer versions while maintaining the same workflow shortcuts.

Could you kindly take a look at these updates and let me know if everything looks good?

Hi @jmschp, good initiative.

It's more complicated to merge though. Ideally, we would need:

- To be backwards compatible with older versions: old alias behavior should still work, I'm not sure if this happens now
- To provide some feedback on what's actually running, to avoid misunderstandings (maybe echo the final command)
- If possible, to provide autocompletion of db names

body:
@mcornella 

> - To be backwards compatible with older versions: old alias behavior should still work, I'm not sure if this happens now

Good catch. There are some problems with backwards compatibility. Some of the commands accept some arguments. I rarely use them so I totally forgot. We could adjust the function to deal with does.

> - To provide some feedback on what's actually running, to avoid misunderstandings (maybe echo the final command)

Sure I can add a echo.

> - If possible, to provide autocompletion of db names

Standard DB names are `primary`, `cache`, `cable` and `queue`. The names are defined at `config/databse.yml`, here is the [Rails MySQL template for a new application](https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/v8.0.3/railties/lib/rails/generators/rails/app/templates/config/databases/mysql.yml.tt). Note that a developer can change the names or add new ones. So I am not sure about auto complete. If a developer is not using the standard names it can be annoying to see them in auto complete. For the task list Rails dynamically fetches the names. Doing the same here could be a bit overkill.

author: carlosala

body:
Fuzzy matching of substrings in the middle and right of completion candidate is a core feature of OMZ. Disabling this doesn't just fix the bug, it turns it off so it doesn't happen.

Before merging this I'd like to understand why it happens, and fix the root cause.

Starting point from this Copilot answer:
```
Short answer
- The matcher "l:|=* r:|=*" creates left‑ and right‑anchored substring matchers that let completion try matches starting at arbitrary offsets inside filenames/words. The completion engine (while building candidate “parts”) used those matchers to construct program/command names that happened to point at a local executable (e.g. bin/test). Those constructed candidates were then passed into the completion driver that invokes completion helper code — which can run the program — so bin/test got executed. Removing the left-side matcher prevents building those spurious candidates.

Key places in zsh source that implement this and how it happens
1) Parsing / installing matchers
- parse_cmatcher / set_gmatcher → compctl.c / complete.c
  - Matcher strings from zstyle/compctl are parsed into internal matcher objects (cmatcher) via parse_cmatcher and stored as a global matcher list (cmatcher / bmatchers).
  - (See: parse_cmatcher and set_gmatcher in Src/Zle/complete.c and Src/Zle/compctl.c.)

2) Adding matchers used when building completion parts
- add_bmatchers / bmatchers list → Src/Zle/compmatch.c
  - The code copies the configured matchers into the bmatchers list used when breaking the input into parts to test for matches.
  - This is where the left/right matcher flags (CMF_LEFT / CMF_RIGHT) and the left/right pattern lengths (llen / ralen) are recorded and later used.

3) Building candidate “parts” using left/right matchers
- bld_parts(...) in Src/Zle/compmatch.c
  - This is the place that actually looks at the bmatchers and tries to match the configured left/right patterns against substrings of the input word:
    - It checks mp->flags for CMF_RIGHT/CMF_LEFT and then calls pattern_match(mp->right, ...) and pattern_match(mp->left, ...) to see whether a candidate match exists at a given offset.
  - When the matcher is effectively “match anything on the left and/or right” (what l:|=* r:|=* does), the condition in bld_parts can succeed for substrings inside filenames/paths — so the engine will build completion candidates from non‑prefix positions in directory entries (for example the filename bin/test).

4) Invoking completion helper code that can execute programs
- runshfunc(...) called inside completion flow → Src/Zle/complete.c and callcompfunc(...) in Src/Zle/compcore.c
  - After building candidates and deciding which completion helper to call, the completion machinery invokes runshfunc(prog, w, name) (complete.c). That code hands off to whichever completion generator is appropriate.
  - If a candidate resolves to a “program” or completion helper that is not purely a safe static list, running that generator can execute external code (shell functions or external programs). callcompfunc and runshfunc are the places that bridge from the completion engine into running completion functions/helpers.
  - In the failure mode caused by the left-side matcher, the constructed candidate string happened to be a path to an executable in the repo (bin/test), so the completion machinery attempted to run it as a helper and thereby executed bin/test.

Why the left-side matcher is the root cause
- The right-side matcher 'r:|=*' alone makes suffix/fuzzy matches (try matches that end at the end of the word) but keeps the matching anchored on one side. The left-side matcher (the 'l:|=*' part) makes the matcher try substrings starting not at the beginning of the word. That extra permissiveness is what allowed the completion engine to treat internal substrings (including a path like bin/test) as valid candidate program names to probe. When the engine probes those candidates it may invoke them as completion helpers, causing execution.

Concrete references (files & functions)
- Src/Zle/complete.c
  - parse_cmatcher / set_gmatcher — parsing matcher-list strings into cmatcher objects
  - call site: runshfunc(prog, w, name) — where the completion generator is invoked
- Src/Zle/compctl.c
  - set_gmatcher / add_bmatchers — placing matchers into the bmatchers list used while building parts
- Src/Zle/compmatch.c
  - bld_parts(...) — uses mp->flags, mp->left/mp->right and calls pattern_match(...) to find matches at offsets; this is the code that allows substring matches when left/right matchers are present
- Src/Zle/compcore.c / Src/Zle/compresult.c
  - do_completion(...) and surrounding code — orchestrates building matches and handing them to completion functions

Summary / practical takeaway
- The left-side matcher allowed completion to construct candidates from substrings (not just prefixes/suffixes). Those candidates included local executable paths (bin/test) which the completion machinery then invoked as completion helpers. The change in the PR removes that left-side matcher so completion no longer builds those spurious candidates and so no longer calls bin/test during tab completion.
```

body:
[We can't accept more themes](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh#do-not-send-us-themes). Please make a separate repo with your theme and link to it from the [external themes](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/External-themes) wiki page.

You can also add your theme as a custom theme. See [customization](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Customization#overriding-and-adding-themes) for instructions.
author: carlosala
